rows.pali: Tato paraṃ "pañcahākārehi anodhisopharaṇā sattahākārehi odhisopharaṇā dasahākārehi disāpharaṇā"ti ayaṃ vikubbanā, "sukhaṃ supatī"tiādayo ānisaṃsā ca mettāyaṃ vuttanayeneva veditabbāti.
rows.english: Next, the versatility consisting in unspecified pervasion in five ways, specified pervasion in seven ways, and directional pervasion in ten ways, and also the advantages described as “He sleeps in comfort,” etc., should be understood in the same way as stated under loving-kindness.

rows.pali: Ayaṃ muditābhāvanāya vitthārakathā.
rows.english: This is the detailed explanation of the development of gladness.

rows.pali: 261.Upekkhābhāvanaṃ bhāvetukāmena pana mettādīsu paṭiladdhatikacatukkajjhānena paguṇatatiyajjhānā vuṭṭhāya "sukhitā hontū"tiādivasena sattakelāyanamanasikārayuttattā, paṭighānunayasamīpacārittā, somanassayogena oḷārikattā ca purimāsu ādīnavaṃ, santasabhāvattā upekkhāya ānisaṃsañca disvā yvāssa pakatimajjhatto puggalo, taṃ ajjhupekkhitvā upekkhā uppādetabbā.
rows.english: 88. One who wants to develop equanimity must have already obtained the triple or quadruple jhāna in loving-kindness, and so on. He should emerge from the third jhāna [in the fourfold reckoning], after he has made it familiar, and he should see danger in the former [three divine abidings] because they are linked with attention given to beings’ enjoyment in the way beginning “May they be happy,” because resentment and approval are near, and because their association with joy is gross. And he should also see the advantage in equanimity because it is peaceful. Then he should arouse equanimity (upekkhā) by looking on with equanimity (ajjhupekkhitvā) at a person who is normally neutral;

rows.pali: Tato piyapuggalādīsu.
rows.english: after that at a dear person, and the rest.

rows.pali: Vuttañhetaṃ "kathañca bhikkhu upekkhāsahagatena cetasā ekaṃ disaṃ pharitvā viharati?
rows.english: For this is said: “And how does a bhikkhu dwell pervading one direction with his heart endued with equanimity?

rows.pali: Seyyathāpi nāma ekaṃ puggalaṃ neva manāpaṃ na amanāpaṃ disvā upekkhako assa, evameva sabbe satte upekkhāya pharatī"ti (vibha. 673).
rows.english: Just as he would feel equanimity on seeing a person who was neither beloved nor unloved, so he pervades all beings with equanimity” (Vibh 275).

rows.pali: Tasmā vuttanayena majjhattapuggale upekkhaṃ uppādetvā atha piyapuggale, tato soṇḍasahāyake, tato verimhīti evaṃ "imesu ca tīsu attani cā"ti sabbattha majjhattavasena sīmāsambhedaṃ katvā taṃ nimittaṃ āsevitabbaṃ bhāvetabbaṃ bahulīkātabbaṃ.
rows.english: 89.Therefore he should arouse equanimity towards the neutral person in the way already stated. Then, through the neutral one, he should break down the barriers in each case between the three people, that is, the dear person, then the boon companion, and then the hostile one, and lastly himself. And he should cultivate that sign, develop and repeatedly practice it.

rows.pali: Tassevaṃ karoto pathavīkasiṇe vuttanayeneva catutthajjhānaṃ uppajjati.
rows.english: 90.As he does so the fourth jhāna arises in him in the way described under the earth kasiṇa.

rows.pali: Kiṃ panetaṃ pathavīkasiṇādīsu uppannatatiyajjhānassāpi uppajjatīti?
rows.english: But how then? Does this arise in one in whom the third jhāna has already arisen on the basis of the earth kasiṇa, etc.?

rows.pali: Nuppajjati.
rows.english: It does not.

rows.pali: Kasmā?
rows.english: Why not?

rows.pali: Ārammaṇavisabhāgatāya.
rows.english: Because of the dissimilarity of the object.

rows.pali: Mettādīsu uppannatatiyajjhānasseva pana uppajjati, ārammaṇasabhāgatāyāti.
rows.english: It arises only in one in whom the third jhāna has arisen on the basis of loving-kindness, etc., because the object is similar.

rows.pali: Tato paraṃ pana vikubbanā ca ānisaṃsapaṭilābho ca mettāyaṃ vuttanayeneva veditabboti.
rows.english: But after that the versatility and the obtaining of advantages should be understood in the same way as described under loving-kindness.

rows.pali: Ayaṃ upekkhābhāvanāya vitthārakathā.
rows.english: This is the detailed explanation of the development of equanimity.

rows.pali: Etāsu hi mettākaruṇāmuditāupekkhāsu atthato tāva mejjatīti mettā, siniyhatīti attho.
rows.english: [Meanings] 92. Now, as to the meaning firstly of loving-kindness, compassion, gladness and equanimity: it fattens (mejjati), thus it is loving-kindness (mettā); it is solvent (siniyhati) is the meaning.

rows.pali: Mitte vā bhavā, mittassa vā esā pavattītipi mettā.
rows.english: Also: it comes about with respect to a friend (mitta), [318] or it is behaviour towards a friend, thus it is loving-kindness (mettā).

rows.pali: Paradukkhe sati sādhūnaṃ hadayakampanaṃ karotīti karuṇā.
rows.english: When there is suffering in others it causes (karoti) good people’s hearts to be moved (kampana), thus it is compassion (karuṇā).

rows.pali: Kiṇāti vā paradukkhaṃ hiṃsati vināsetīti karuṇā.
rows.english: Or alternatively, it combats (kiṇāti)11 others’ suffering, attacks and demolishes it, thus it is compassion.

rows.pali: Kiriyati vā dukkhitesu pharaṇavasena pasāriyatīti karuṇā.
rows.english: Or alternatively, it is scattered (kiriyati) upon those who suffer, it is extended to them by pervasion, thus it is compassion (karuṇā).

rows.pali: Modanti tāya taṃsamaṅgino, sayaṃ vā modati, modanamattameva vā tanti muditā.
rows.english: Those endowed with it are glad (modanti), or itself is glad (modati), or it is the mere act of being glad (modana), thus it is gladness (muditā).

rows.pali: "Averā hontū"tiādibyāpārappahānena majjhattabhāvūpagamanena ca upekkhatīti upekkhā.
rows.english: It looks on at (upekkhati), abandoning such interestedness as thinking “May they be free from enmity” and having recourse to neutrality, thus it is equanimity (upekkhā).

rows.pali: 263.Lakkhaṇādito panettha hitākārappavattilakkhaṇā mettā, hitūpasaṃhārarasā, āghātavinayapaccupaṭṭhānā, sattānaṃ manāpabhāvadassanapadaṭṭhānā.
rows.english: [Characteristic, Etc.] 93. As to the characteristic, etc., loving-kindness is characterized here as promoting the aspect of welfare. Its function is to prefer welfare. It is manifested as the removal of annoyance. Its proximate cause is seeing loveableness in beings.

rows.pali: Byāpādūpasamo etissā sampatti, sinehasambhavo vipatti.
rows.english: It succeeds when it makes ill will subside, and it fails when it produces (selfish) affection.

rows.pali: Dukkhāpanayanākārappavattilakkhaṇā karuṇā, paradukkhāsahanarasā, avihiṃsāpaccupaṭṭhānā, dukkhābhibhūtānaṃ anāthabhāvadassanapadaṭṭhānā.
rows.english: 94.Compassion is characterized as promoting the aspect of allaying suffering. Its function resides in not bearing others’ suffering. It is manifested as non- cruelty. Its proximate cause is to see helplessness in those overwhelmed by suffering.

rows.pali: Vihiṃsūpasamo tassā sampatti, sokasambhavo vipatti.
rows.english: It succeeds when it makes cruelty subside and it fails when it produces sorrow.

rows.pali: Pamodanalakkhaṇā muditā, anissāyanarasā, arativighātapaccupaṭṭhānā, sattānaṃ sampattidassanapadaṭṭhānā.
rows.english: 95. Gladness is characterized as gladdening (produced by others’ success).12 Its function resides in being unenvious. It is manifested as the elimination of aversion (boredom). Its proximate cause is seeing beings, success.

rows.pali: Arativūpasamo tassā sampatti, pahāsasambhavo vipatti.
rows.english: It succeeds when it makes aversion (boredom) subside, and it fails when it produces merriment.

rows.pali: Sattesu majjhattākārappavattilakkhaṇā upekkhā, sattesu samabhāvadassanarasā, paṭighānunayavūpasamapaccupaṭṭhānā, "kammassakā sattā, te kassa ruciyā sukhitā vā bhavissanti, dukkhato vā muccissanti, pattasampattito vā na parihāyissantī"ti evaṃ pavattakammassakatādassanapadaṭṭhānā.
rows.english: 96.Equanimity is characterized as promoting the aspect of neutrality towards beings. Its function is to see equality in beings. It is manifested as the quieting of resentment and approval. Its proximate cause is seeing ownership of deeds (kamma) thus: “Beings are owners of their deeds. Whose13 [if not theirs] is the choice by which they will become happy, or will get free from suffering, or will not fall away from the success they have reached?

rows.pali: Paṭighānunayavūpasamo tassā sampatti, gehasitāya aññāṇupekkhāya sambhavo vipatti.
rows.english: ” It succeeds when it makes resentment and approval subside, and it fails when it produces the equanimity of unknowing, which is that [worldly-minded indifference of ignorance] based on the house life.

rows.pali: 264.Catunnampi panetesaṃ brahmavihārānaṃ vipassanāsukhañceva bhavasampatti ca sādhāraṇappayojanaṃ.
rows.english: [Purpose] 97. The general purpose of these four divine abidings is the bliss of insight and an excellent [form of future] existence.

rows.pali: Byāpādādipaṭighāto āveṇikaṃ.
rows.english: That peculiar to each is respectively the warding off of ill will, and so on.

rows.pali: Byāpādapaṭighātappayojanā hettha mettā.
rows.english: For here loving-kindness has the purpose of warding off ill will,

rows.pali: Vihiṃsāaratirāgapaṭighātappayojanā itarā.
rows.english: while the others have the respective purposes of warding off cruelty, aversion (boredom), and greed or resentment.

rows.pali: Vuttampi cetaṃ –
rows.english: And this is said too:

rows.pali: "Nissaraṇañhetaṃ, āvuso, byāpādassa yadidaṃ mettā cetovimutti.
rows.english: “For this is the escape from ill will, friends, that is to say, the mind-deliverance of loving-kindness …

rows.pali: Nissaraṇañhetaṃ, āvuso, vihesāya yadidaṃ karuṇā cetovimutti.
rows.english: For this is the escape from cruelty, friends, that is to say, the mind-deliverance of compassion …

rows.pali: Nissaraṇañhetaṃ, āvuso, aratiyā yadidaṃ muditā cetovimutti.
rows.english: For this is the escape from boredom, friends, that is to say, the mind-deliverance of gladness …

rows.pali: Nissaraṇañhetaṃ, āvuso, rāgassa yadidaṃ upekkhā cetovimuttī"ti (dī. ni. 3.326; a. ni. 6.13).
rows.english: For this is the escape from greed, friends, that is to say, the mind-deliverance of equanimity” (D III 248).

rows.pali: 265.Ekekassa cettha āsannadūravasena dve dve paccatthikā.
rows.english: [The Near and Far Enemies] 98.And here each one has two enemies, one near and one far.

rows.pali: Mettābrahmavihārassa hi samīpacāro viya purisassa sapatto guṇadassanasabhāgatāya rāgo āsannapaccatthiko, so lahuṃ otāraṃ labhati, tasmā tato suṭṭhu mettā rakkhitabbā.
rows.english: The divine abiding of loving-kindness [319] has greed as its near enemy,14 since both share in seeing virtues. Greed behaves like a foe who keeps close by a man, and it easily finds an opportunity. So loving-kindness should be well protected from it.

rows.pali: Pabbatādigahananissito viya purisassa sapatto sabhāgavisabhāgatāya byāpādo dūrapaccatthiko, tasmā tato nibbhayena mettāyitabbaṃ.
rows.english: And ill will, which is dissimilar to the similar greed, is its far enemy like a foe ensconced in a rock wilderness. So loving-kindness must be practiced free from fear of that;

rows.pali: Mettāyissati ca nāma, kopañca karissatīti aṭṭhānametaṃ.
rows.english: for it is not possible to practice loving-kindness and feel anger simultaneously (see D III 247–48).

rows.pali: Karuṇābrahmavihārassa "cakkhuviññeyyānaṃ rūpānaṃ iṭṭhānaṃ kantānaṃ manāpānaṃ manoramānaṃ lokāmisapaṭisaṃyuttānaṃ appaṭilābhaṃ vā appaṭilābhato samanupassato pubbe vā paṭiladdhapubbaṃ atītaṃ niruddhaṃ vipariṇataṃ samanussarato uppajjati domanassaṃ, yaṃ evarūpaṃ domanassaṃ, idaṃ vuccati gehasitaṃ domanassa"ntiādinā (ma. ni. 3.307) nayena āgataṃ gehasitaṃ domanassaṃ vipattidassanasabhāgatāya āsannapaccatthikaṃ.
rows.english: 99.Compassion has grief based on the home life as its near enemy, since both share in seeing failure. Such grief has been described in the way beginning, “When a man either regards as a privation failure to obtain visible objects cognizable by the eye that are sought after, desired, agreeable, gratifying and associated with worldliness, or when he recalls those formerly obtained that are past, ceased and changed, then grief arises in him. Such grief as this is called grief based on the home life” (M III 218).

rows.pali: Sabhāgavisabhāgatāya vihiṃsā dūrapaccatthikā.
rows.english: And cruelty, which is dissimilar to the similar grief, is its far enemy.

rows.pali: Tasmā tato nibbhayena karuṇāyitabbaṃ.
rows.english: So compassion must be practiced free from fear of that;

rows.pali: Karuṇañca nāma karissati, pāṇiādīhi ca viheṭhissatīti aṭṭhānametaṃ.
rows.english: for it is not possible to practice compassion and be cruel to breathing things simultaneously.

rows.pali: Muditābrahmavihārassa "cakkhuviññeyyānaṃ rūpānaṃ iṭṭhānaṃ - pe - lokāmisapaṭisaṃyuttānaṃ paṭilābhaṃ vā paṭilābhato samanupassato pubbe vā paṭiladdhapubbaṃ atītaṃ niruddhaṃ vipariṇataṃ samanussarato uppajjati somanassaṃ, yaṃ evarūpaṃ somanassaṃ, idaṃ vuccati gehasitaṃ somanassa"ntiādinā (ma. ni. 3.306) nayena āgataṃ gehasitaṃ somanassaṃ sampattidassanasabhāgatāya āsannapaccatthikaṃ, sabhāgavisabhāgatāya arati dūrapaccatthikā.
rows.english: 100. Gladness has joy based on the home life as its near enemy, since both share in seeing success. Such joy has been described in the way beginning, “When a man either regards as gain the obtaining of visible objects cognizable by the eye that are sought … and associated with worldliness, or recalls those formerly obtained that are past, ceased, and changed, then joy arises in him. Such joy as this is called joy based on the home life” (M III 217). And aversion (boredom), which is dissimilar to the similar joy, is its far enemy.

rows.pali: Tasmā tato nibbhayena muditā bhāvetabbā.
rows.english: So gladness should be practiced free from fear of that;

rows.pali: Mudito ca nāma bhavissati, pantasenāsanesu ca adhikusalesu dhammesu vā ukkaṇṭhissatīti aṭṭhānametaṃ.
rows.english: for it is not possible to practice gladness and be discontented with remote abodes and things connected with the higher profitableness simultaneously.

rows.pali: Upekkhābrahmavihārassa pana "cakkhunā rūpaṃ disvā uppajjati upekkhā bālassa mūḷhassa puthujjanassa anodhijinassa avipākajinassa anādīnavadassāvino assutavato puthujjanassa yā evarūpā upekkhā, rūpaṃ sā nātivattati.
rows.english: 101. Such unknowing has been described in the way beginning, “On seeing a visible object with the eye equanimity arises in the foolish infatuated ordinary man, in the untaught ordinary man who has not conquered his limitations, who has not conquered future [kamma] result, who is unperceiving of danger. Such equanimity as this does not surmount the visible object.

rows.pali: Tasmā sā upekkhā gehasitāti vuccatī"tiādinā (ma. ni. 3.308) nayena āgatā gehasitā aññāṇupekkhā dosaguṇāvicāraṇavasena sabhāgattā āsannapaccatthikā.
rows.english: Such equanimity as this is called equanimity based on the home life” (M III 219). Equanimity has the equanimity of unknowing (above) based on the home life as its near enemy, since both share in ignoring faults and virtues.

rows.pali: Sabhāgavisabhāgatāya rāgapaṭighā dūrapaccatthikā.
rows.english: And greed and resentment, which are dissimilar to the similar unknowing, are its far enemies.

rows.pali: Tasmā tato nibbhayena upekkhitabbaṃ.
rows.english: Therefore equanimity must be practiced free from fear of that;

rows.pali: Upekkhissati ca nāma, rajjissati ca paṭihaññissati cāti aṭṭhānametaṃ.
rows.english: for it is not possible to look on with equanimity and be inflamed with greed or be resentful15 simultaneously.

rows.pali: 266.Sabbesampi ca etesaṃ kattukāmatā chando ādi, nīvaraṇādivikkhambhanaṃ majjhaṃ, appanā pariyosānaṃ.
rows.english: [The Beginning, Middle and End, Etc.] 102. Now, zeal consisting in desire to act is the beginning of all these things. Suppression of the hindrances, etc., is the middle. Absorption is the end.

rows.pali: Paññattidhammavasena eko vā satto aneke vā sattā ārammaṇaṃ.
rows.english: Their object is a single living being or many living beings, as a mental object consisting in a concept.

rows.pali: Upacāre vā appanāya vā pattāya ārammaṇavaḍḍhanaṃ.
rows.english: [The Order in Extension] 103. The extension of the object takes place either in access or in absorption.

rows.pali: Tatrāyaṃ vaḍḍhanakkamo, yathā hi kusalo kassako kasitabbaṭṭhānaṃ paricchinditvā kasati, evaṃ paṭhamameva ekamāvāsaṃ paricchinditvā tattha sattesu imasmiṃ āvāse sattā averā hontūtiādinā nayena mettā bhāvetabbā.
rows.english: Here is the order of it. Just as a skilled ploughman first delimits an area and then does his ploughing, so first a single dwelling should be delimited and loving- kindness developed towards all beings there in the way beginning, “In this dwelling may all beings be free from enmity.”

rows.pali: Tattha cittaṃ muduṃ kammaniyaṃ katvā dve āvāsā paricchinditabbā.
rows.english: When his mind has become malleable and wieldy with respect to that, he can then delimit two dwellings.

rows.pali: Tato anukkamena tayo, cattāro, pañca, cha, satta, aṭṭha, nava, dasa, ekā racchā, upaḍḍhagāmo, gāmo, janapado, rajjaṃ, ekā disāti evaṃ yāva ekaṃ cakkavāḷaṃ, tato vā pana bhiyyo tattha tattha sattesu mettā bhāvetabbā.
rows.english: Next he can successively delimit three, four, five, six, seven, eight, nine, ten, one street, half the village, the whole village, the district, the kingdom, one direction, and so on up to one world-sphere, or even beyond that, and develop loving- kindness towards the beings in such areas.

rows.pali: Tathā karuṇādayoti ayamettha ārammaṇavaḍḍhanakkamo.
rows.english: Likewise with compassion and so on. This is the order in extending here.

rows.pali: 267.Yathā pana kasiṇānaṃ nissando āruppā, samādhinissando nevasaññānāsaññāyatanaṃ, vipassanānissando phalasamāpatti, samathavipassanānissando nirodhasamāpatti, evaṃ purimabrahmavihārattayanissando ettha upekkhābrahmavihāro.
rows.english: [The Outcome] 104. Just as the immaterial states are the outcome of the kasiṇas, and the base consisting of neither perception nor non-perception is the outcome of concentration, and fruition attainment is the outcome of insight, and the attainment of cessation is the outcome of serenity coupled with insight, so the divine abiding of equanimity is the outcome of the first three divine abidings.

rows.pali: Yathā hi thambhe anussāpetvā tulāsaṅghāṭaṃ anāropetvā na sakkā ākāse kūṭagopānasiyo ṭhapetuṃ, evaṃ purimesu tatiyajjhānaṃ vinā na sakkā catutthaṃ bhāvetunti.
rows.english: For just as the gable rafters cannot be placed in the air without having first set up the scaffolding and built the framework of beams, so it is not possible to develop the fourth (jhāna in the fourth divine abiding) without having already developed the third jhāna in the earlier (three divine abidings).

rows.pali: 268.Ettha siyā, kasmā panetā mettākaruṇāmuditāupekkhā brahmavihārāti vuccanti?
rows.english: [Four Questions] 105. And here it may be asked: But why are loving-kindness, compassion, gladness, and equanimity, called divine abidings?

rows.pali: Kasmā ca catassova?
rows.english: And why are they only four?

rows.pali: Ko ca etāsaṃ kamo, abhidhamme ca kasmā appamaññāti vuttāti?
rows.english: And what is their order? And why are they called measureless states in the Abhidhamma?

rows.pali: Vuccate, seṭṭhaṭṭhena tāva niddosabhāvena cettha brahmavihāratā veditabbā.
rows.english: 106.It may be replied: The divineness of the abiding (brahmavihāratā) should be understood here in the sense of best and in the sense of immaculate.

rows.pali: Sattesu sammāpaṭipattibhāvena hi seṭṭhā ete vihārā.
rows.english: For these abidings are the best in being the right attitude towards beings.

rows.pali: Yathā ca brahmāno niddosacittā viharanti, evaṃ etehi sampayuttā yogino brahmasamā hutvā viharantīti seṭṭhaṭṭhena niddosabhāvena ca brahmavihārāti vuccanti.
rows.english: And just as Brahmā gods abide with immaculate minds, so the meditators who associate themselves with these abidings abide on an equal footing with Brahmā gods. So they are called divine abidings in the sense of best and in the sense of immaculate.

rows.pali: 269.Kasmā ca catassovātiādi pañhassa pana idaṃ vissajjanaṃ.
rows.english: 107. Here are the answers to the questions beginning with “Why are they only four? ”:

rows.pali: Etāsu hi yasmā mettā byāpādabahulassa, karuṇā vihesābahulassa, muditā aratibahulassa, upekkhā rāgabahulassa visuddhimaggo.
rows.english: 108. For among these, loving-kindness is the way to purity for one who has much ill will, compassion is that for one who has much cruelty, gladness is that for one who has much aversion (boredom), and equanimity is that for one who has much greed.

rows.pali: Yasmā ca hitūpasaṃhāraahitāpanayanasampattimodanaanābhogavasena catubbidhoyeva sattesu manasikāro.
rows.english: Also attention given to beings is only fourfold, that is to say, as bringing welfare, as removing suffering, as being glad at their success, and as unconcern, [that is to say, impartial neutrality].

rows.pali: Yasmā ca yathā mātā daharagilānayobbanappattasakiccapasutesu catūsu puttesu daharassa abhivuḍḍhikāmā hoti, gilānassa gelaññāpanayanakāmā, yobbanappattassa yobbanasampattiyā ciraṭṭhitikāmā, sakakiccapasutassa kismiñci pariyāye abyāvaṭā hoti, tathā appamaññāvihārikenāpi sabbasattesu mettādivasena bhavitabbaṃ.
rows.english: And one abiding in the measureless states should practice loving-kindness and the rest like a mother with four sons, namely, a child, an invalid, one in the flush of youth, and one busy with his own affairs; for she wants the child to grow up, wants the invalid to get well, wants the one in the flush of youth to enjoy for long the benefits of youth, and is not at all bothered about the one who is busy with his own affairs.

rows.pali: Tasmā ito visuddhimaggādivasā catassova appamaññā.
rows.english: That is why the measureless states are only four as “due to paths to purity and other sets of four.”

rows.pali: Yasmā catassopetā bhāvetukāmena paṭhamaṃ hitākārappavattivasena sattesu paṭipajjitabbaṃ, hitākārappavattilakkhaṇā ca mettā.
rows.english: 109. One who wants to develop these four should practice them towards beings first as the promotion of the aspect of welfare—and loving-kindness has the promotion of the aspect of welfare as its characteristic;

rows.pali: Tato evaṃ patthitahitānaṃ sattānaṃ dukkhābhibhavaṃ disvā vā sutvā vā sambhāvetvā vā dukkhāpanayanākārappavattivasena, dukkhāpanayanākārappavattilakkhaṇā ca karuṇā.
rows.english: and next, on seeing or hearing or judging16 that beings whose welfare has been thus wished for are at the mercy of suffering, they should be practiced as the promotion of the aspect of the removal of suffering—and compassion has the promotion of the aspect of the removal of suffering as its characteristic;

rows.pali: Athevaṃ patthitahitānaṃ patthitadukkhāpagamānañca nesaṃ sampattiṃ disvā sampattipamodanavasena, pamodanalakkhaṇā ca muditā.
rows.english: and then, on seeing the success of those whose welfare has been wished for and the removal of whose suffering has been wished for, they should be practiced as being glad—and gladness has the act of gladdening as its characteristic;

rows.pali: Tato paraṃ pana kattabbābhāvato ajjhupekkhakattasaṅkhātena majjhattākārena paṭipajjitabbaṃ, majjhattākārappavattilakkhaṇā ca upekkhā.
rows.english: but after that there is nothing to be done and so they should be practiced as the neutral aspect, in other words, the state of an onlooker—and equanimity has the promotion of the aspect of neutrality as its characteristic;

rows.pali: Tasmā ito hitādiākāravasā panāsaṃ paṭhamaṃ mettā vuttā, atha karuṇā muditā upekkhāti ayaṃ kamo veditabbo.
rows.english: therefore, since their respective aims are the aspect of welfare, etc., their order should be understood to correspond, with loving- kindness stated first, then compassion, gladness and equanimity.

rows.pali: Yasmā pana sabbāpetā appamāṇe gocare pavattanti.
rows.english: 110.All of them, however, occur with a measureless scope,

rows.pali: Appamāṇā hi sattā etāsaṃ gocarabhūtā.
rows.english: for their scope is measureless beings;

rows.pali: Ekasattassāpi ca ettake padese mettādayo bhāvetabbāti evaṃ pamāṇaṃ agahetvā sakalapharaṇavaseneva pavattāti.
rows.english: and instead of assuming a measure such as “Loving- kindness, etc., should be developed only towards a single being, or in an area of such an extent,” they occur with universal pervasion.

rows.pali: Tena vuttaṃ –
rows.english: That is why it was said:

rows.pali: 270.Evaṃ appamāṇagocaratāya ekalakkhaṇāsu cāpi etāsu purimā tisso tikacatukkajjhānikāva honti.
rows.english: [As Producing Three Jhānas and Four Jhānas] 111.Though they have a single characteristic in having a measureless scope, yet the first three are only of triple and quadruple jhāna [respectively in the fourfold and fivefold reckonings].

rows.pali: Kasmā?
rows.english: Why?

rows.pali: Somanassāvippayogato.
rows.english: Because they are not dissociated from joy.

rows.pali: Kasmā panāyaṃ somanassena avippayogoti?
rows.english: But why are their aims not dissociated from joy?

rows.pali: Domanassasamuṭṭhitānaṃ byāpādādīnaṃ nissaraṇattā.
rows.english: Because they are the escape from ill will, etc., which are originated by grief.

rows.pali: Pacchimā pana avasesaekajjhānikāva.
rows.english: But the last one belongs only to the remaining single jhāna.

rows.pali: Kasmā?
rows.english: Why?

rows.pali: Upekkhāvedanāsampayogato.
rows.english: Because it is associated with equanimous feeling.

rows.pali: Na hi sattesu majjhattākārappavattā brahmavihārupekkhā upekkhāvedanaṃ vinā vattatīti.
rows.english: For the divine abiding of equanimity that occurs in the aspect of neutrality towards beings does not exist apart from equanimous [that is to say, neither- painful-nor-pleasant] feeling.

rows.pali: 271.Yo panevaṃ vadeyya "yasmā bhagavatā aṭṭhakanipāte catūsupi appamaññāsu avisesena vuttaṃ 'tato tvaṃ bhikkhu imaṃ samādhiṃ savitakkampi savicāraṃ bhāveyyāsi, avitakkampi vicāramattaṃ bhāveyyāsi, avitakkampi avicāraṃ bhāveyyāsi, sappītikampi bhāveyyāsi, nippītikampi bhāveyyāsi, sātasahagatampi bhāveyyāsi, upekkhāsahagatampi bhāveyyāsī'ti (a. ni. 8.63), tasmā catasso appamaññā catukkapañcakajjhānikā"ti.
rows.english: 112. However, someone might say this: “It has been said by the Blessed One in the Book of Eights, speaking of the measureless states in general: ‘Next, bhikkhu, you should develop the concentration with applied thought and sustained thought, and you should develop it without applied thought and with sustained thought only, and you should develop it without applied thought and without sustained thought, and you should develop it with happiness, and you should develop it without happiness, and you should develop it accompanied by gratification, and you should develop it accompanied by equanimity’ (A IV 300). Consequently all four measureless states have quadruple and quintuple jhāna.”

rows.pali: So māhevantissa vacanīyo.
rows.english: 113. He should be told: “Do not put it like that.

rows.pali: Evañhi sati kāyānupassanādayopi catukkapañcakajjhānikā siyuṃ, vedanādīsu ca paṭhamajjhānampi natthi, pageva dutiyādīni.
rows.english: For if that were so, then contemplation of the body, etc., would also have quadruple and quintuple jhāna. But there is not even the first jhāna in the contemplation of feeling or in the other two.17

rows.pali: Tasmā byañjanacchāyāmattaṃ gahetvā mā bhagavantaṃ abbhācikkhi, gambhīraṃ hi buddhavacanaṃ, taṃ ācariye payirupāsitvā adhippāyato gahetabbaṃ.
rows.english: So do not misrepresent the Blessed One by adherence to the letter. The Enlightened One’s word is profound and should be taken as it is intended, giving due weight to the teachers.”

rows.pali: 272.Ayañhi tatrādhippāyo – "sādhu me, bhante, bhagavā saṃkhittena dhammaṃ desetu, yamahaṃ bhagavato dhammaṃ sutvā eko vūpakaṭṭho appamatto ātāpī pahitatto vihareyya"nti evaṃ āyācitadhammadesanaṃ kira taṃ bhikkhuṃ yasmā so pubbepi dhammaṃ sutvā tattheva vasati, na samaṇadhammaṃ kātuṃ gacchati, tasmā naṃ bhagavā "evameva panidhekacce moghapurisā mamaññeva ajjhesanti, dhamme ca bhāsite mamaññeva anubandhitabbaṃ maññantī"ti apasādetvā puna yasmā so arahattassa upanissayasampanno, tasmā naṃ ovadanto āha – "tasmātiha te bhikkhu evaṃ sikkhitabbaṃ, ajjhattaṃ me cittaṃ ṭhitaṃ bhavissati susaṇṭhitaṃ, na cuppannā pāpakā akusalā dhammā cittaṃ pariyādāya ṭhassantīti.
rows.english: 114. And the intention here is this: The Blessed One, it seems, was asked to teach the Dhamma thus: “Venerable sir, it would be good if the Blessed One would teach me the Dhamma in brief, so that, having heard the Blessed One’s Dhamma, I may dwell alone, withdrawn, diligent, ardent and self-exerted” (A IV 299). But the Blessed One had no confidence yet in that bhikkhu, since although he had already heard the Dhamma he had nevertheless gone on living there instead of going to do the ascetic’s duties, [and the Blessed One expressed his lack of confidence] thus: “So too, some misguided men merely question me, and when the Dhamma is expounded [to them], they still fancy that they need not follow me” (A IV 299). However, the bhikkhu had the potentiality for the attainment of Arahantship, and so he advised him again, [323] saying: “Therefore, bhikkhu, you should train thus: ‘My mind shall be steadied, quite steadied internally, and arisen evil unprofitable things shall not obsess my mind and remain.’

rows.pali: Evañhi te bhikkhu sikkhitabba"nti.
rows.english: You should train thus” (A IV 299).

rows.pali: Iminā panassa ovādena niyakajjhattavasena cittekaggatāmatto mūlasamādhi vutto.
rows.english: But what is stated in that advice is basic concentration consisting in mere unification of mind18 internally in the sense of in oneself (see Ch. XIV, n. 75).

rows.pali: Tato "ettakeneva santuṭṭhiṃ anāpajjitvā evaṃ so eva samādhi vaḍḍhetabbo"ti dassetuṃ "yato kho te bhikkhu ajjhattaṃ cittaṃ ṭhitaṃ hoti susaṇṭhitaṃ, na cuppannā pāpakā akusalā dhammā cittaṃ pariyādāya tiṭṭhanti.
rows.english: 115. After that he told him about its development by means of loving-kindness in order to show that he should not rest content with just that much but should intensify his basic concentration in this way: “As soon as your mind has become steadied, quite steadied internally, bhikkhu, and arisen evil unprofitable things do not obsess your mind and remain,

rows.pali: Tato te bhikkhu evaṃ sikkhitabbaṃ mettā me cetovimutti bhāvitā bhavissati bahulīkatā yānikatā vatthukatā anuṭṭhitā paricitā susamāraddhāti.
rows.english: then you should train thus: ‘The mind- deliverance of loving-kindness will be developed by me, frequently practiced, made the vehicle, made the foundation, established, consolidated, and properly undertaken.’

rows.pali: Evañhi te bhikkhu sikkhitabba"nti evamassa mettāvasena bhāvanaṃ vatvā puna "yato kho te bhikkhu ayaṃ samādhi evaṃ bhāvito hoti bahulīkato, tato tvaṃ bhikkhu imaṃ mūlasamādhiṃ savitakkampi savicāraṃ bhāveyyāsi - pe - upekkhāsahagatampi bhāveyyāsī"ti vuttaṃ.
rows.english: You should train thus, bhikkhu” (A IV 299–300), after which he said further: “As soon as this concentration has been thus developed by you, bhikkhu,19 and frequently practiced, then you should develop this concentration with applied thought and sustained thought … and you should develop it accompanied by equanimity” (A IV 300).

rows.pali: Tassattho – yadā te bhikkhu ayaṃ mūlasamādhi evaṃ mettāvasena bhāvito hoti, tadā tvaṃ tāvatakenāpi tuṭṭhiṃ anāpajjitvāva imaṃ mūlasamādhiṃ aññesupi ārammaṇesu catukkapañcakajjhānāni pāpayamāno savitakkampi savicārantiādinā nayena bhāveyyāsīti.
rows.english: 116. The meaning is this: “Bhikkhu, when this basic concentration has been developed by you by means of loving-kindness, then, instead of resting content with just that much, you should make this basic concentration reach quadruple and quintuple jhāna in other objects by [further] developing it in the way beginning ‘With applied thought.’ ”

rows.pali: Evaṃ vatvā ca puna karuṇādiavasesabrahmavihārapubbaṅgamampissa aññesu ārammaṇesu catukkapañcakajjhānavasena bhāvanaṃ kareyyāsīti dassento "yato kho te bhikkhu ayaṃ samādhi evaṃ bhāvito hoti bahulīkato.
rows.english: 117. And having spoken thus, pointing out that “you should effect its [further] development by means of quadruple and quintuple jhāna in other objects, this [further] development being preceded by the remaining divine abidings of compassion and the rest.” he further said: “As soon as this concentration has been thus developed by you, bhikkhu, and frequently practiced,

rows.pali: Tato te bhikkhu evaṃ sikkhitabbaṃ karuṇā me cetovimuttī"tiādimāha.
rows.english: then you should train thus: ‘The mind-deliverance of compassion will be developed by me …’ (A IV 300), etc.

rows.pali: Evaṃ mettādipubbaṅgamaṃ catukkapañcakajjhānavasena bhāvanaṃ dassetvā puna kāyānupassanādipubbaṅgamaṃ dassetuṃ "yato kho te bhikkhu ayaṃ samādhi evaṃ bhāvito hoti bahulīkato, tato te bhikkhu evaṃ sikkhitabbaṃ kāye kāyānupassī viharissāmī"ti ādiṃ vatvā "yato kho te bhikkhu ayaṃ samādhi evaṃ bhāvito bhavissati subhāvito, tato tvaṃ bhikkhu yena yeneva gagghasi, phāsuññeva gagghasi, yattha yattheva ṭhassasi, phāsuññeva ṭhassasi, yattha yattheva nisīdissasi, phāsuññeva nisīdissasi, yattha yattheva seyyaṃ kappessasi, phāsuññeva seyyaṃ kappessasī"ti arahattanikūṭena desanaṃ samāpesi.
rows.english: 118. Having thus shown how its [further] development by means of quadruple and quintuple jhāna is preceded by loving-kindness, etc., and having told him, “As soon as this concentration has been developed by you, bhikkhu, and frequently practiced, then you should train thus: ‘I shall dwell contemplating the body as a body,’” etc., he concluded the discourse with Arahantship as its culmination thus: “As soon as this concentration has been developed by you, bhikkhu, completely developed, then wherever you go you will go in comfort, wherever you stand you will stand in comfort, wherever [324] you sit you will sit in comfort, wherever you make your couch you will do so in comfort” (A IV 301).

rows.pali: Tasmā tikacatukkajjhānikāva mettādayo, upekkhā pana avasesaekajjhānikāvāti veditabbā.
rows.english: From that it must be understood that the [three] beginning with loving-kindness have only triple-quadruple jhāna, and that equanimity has only the single remaining jhāna.

rows.pali: Tatheva ca abhidhamme (dha. sa. 251 ādayo; vibha. 673 ādayo) vibhattāti.
rows.english: And they are expounded in the same way in the Abhidhamma as well.

rows.pali: 273.Evaṃ tikacatukkajjhānavasena ceva avasesaekajjhānavasena ca dvidhā ṭhitānampi etāsaṃ subhaparamādivasena aññamaññaṃ asadiso ānubhāvaviseso veditabbo.
rows.english: [The Highest Limit of Each] 119. And while they are twofold by way of the triple-quadruple jhāna and the single remaining jhāna, still they should be understood to be distinguishable in each case

rows.pali: Haliddavasanasuttasmiṃ hi etā subhaparamādibhāvena visesetvā vuttā.
rows.english: by a different efficacy consisting in having “beauty as the highest,” etc. For they are so described in the Haliddavasana Sutta,

rows.pali: Yathāha – "subhaparamāhaṃ, bhikkhave, mettaṃ cetovimuttiṃ vadāmi.
rows.english: according as it is said: “Bhikkhus, the mind-deliverance of loving-kindness has beauty as the highest, I say …

rows.pali: Ākāsānañcāyatanaparamāhaṃ, bhikkhave, karuṇaṃ cetovimuttiṃ vadāmi.
rows.english: The mind-deliverance of compassion has the base consisting of boundless space as the highest, I say …

rows.pali: Viññāṇañcāyatanaparamāhaṃ, bhikkhave, muditaṃ cetovimuttiṃ vadāmi.
rows.english: The mind-deliverance of gladness has the base consisting of boundless consciousness as the highest I say …

rows.pali: Ākiñcaññāyatanaparamāhaṃ, bhikkhave, upekkhaṃ cetovimuttiṃ vadāmī"ti (saṃ. ni. 5.235).
rows.english: The mind- deliverance of equanimity has the base consisting of nothingness as the highest, I say” (S V 119–21).20

rows.pali: Kasmā panetā evaṃ vuttāti?
rows.english: 120. But why are they described in this way?

rows.pali: Tassa tassa upanissayattā.
rows.english: Because each is the respective basic support for each.

rows.pali: Mettāvihārissa hi sattā appaṭikkūlā honti.
rows.english: For beings are unrepulsive to one who abides in loving- kindness.

rows.pali: Athassa appaṭikkūlaparicayā appaṭikkūlesu parisuddhavaṇṇesu nīlādīsu cittaṃ upasaṃharato appakasireneva tattha cittaṃ pakkhandati.
rows.english: Being familiar with the unrepulsive aspect, when he applies his mind to unrepulsive pure colours such as blue-black, his mind enters into them without difficulty.

rows.pali: Iti mettā subhavimokkhassa upanissayo hoti, na tato paraṃ, tasmā subhaparamāti vuttā.
rows.english: So loving-kindness is the basic support for the liberation by the beautiful (see M II 12; M-a III 256), but not for what is beyond that. That is why it is called “having beauty as the highest.”

rows.pali: Karuṇāvihārissa pana daṇḍābhighātādirūpanimittaṃ pattadukkhaṃ samanupassantassa karuṇāya pavattisambhavato rūpe ādīnavo parividito hoti.
rows.english: 121. One who abides in compassion has come to know thoroughly the danger in materiality, since compassion is aroused in him when he sees the suffering of beings that has as its material sign (cause) beating with sticks, and so on.

rows.pali: Athassa parividitarūpādīnavattā pathavīkasiṇādīsu aññataraṃ ugghāṭetvā rūpanissaraṇe ākāse cittaṃ upasaṃharato appakasireneva tattha cittaṃ pakkhandati.
rows.english: So, well knowing the danger in materiality, when he removes whichever kasiṇa [concept he was contemplating], whether that of the earth kasiṇa or another, and applies his mind to the space [that remains (see X.6)], which is the escape from materiality, then his mind enters into that [space] without difficulty.

rows.pali: Iti karuṇā ākāsānañcāyatanassa upanissayo hoti, na tato paraṃ, tasmā ākāsānañcāyatanaparamāti vuttā.
rows.english: So compassion is the basic support for the sphere of boundless space, but not for what is beyond that. That is why it is called “having the base consisting of boundless space as the highest.”

rows.pali: Muditāvihārissa pana tena tena pāmojjakāraṇena uppannapāmojjasattānaṃ viññāṇaṃ samanupassantassa muditāya pavattisambhavato viññāṇaggahaṇaparicitaṃ cittaṃ hoti.
rows.english: 122. When he abides in gladness, his mind becomes familiar with apprehending consciousness, since gladness is aroused in him when he sees beings’ consciousness arisen in the form of rejoicing over some reason for joy.

rows.pali: Athassa anukkamādhigataṃ ākāsānañcāyatanaṃ atikkamma ākāsanimittagocare viññāṇe cittaṃ upasaṃharato appakasireneva tattha cittaṃ pakkhandatīti muditā viññāṇañcāyatanassa upanissayo hoti, na tato paraṃ, tasmā viññāṇañcāyatanaparamāti vuttā.
rows.english: Then when he surmounts the sphere of boundless space that he had already attained in due course and applies his mind to the consciousness that had as its object the sign of space, [325] his mind enters into it without difficulty. So gladness is the basic support for the base consisting of boundless consciousness, but not for what is beyond that. That is why it is called “having the sphere of boundless consciousness as the highest.”

rows.pali: Upekkhāvihārissa pana "sattā sukhitā vā hontu dukkhato vā vimuccantu, sampattasukhato vā mā vimuccantū"ti ābhogābhāvato sukhadukkhādiparamatthagāhavimukhabhāvato avijjamānaggahaṇadukkhaṃ cittaṃ hoti.
rows.english: 123. When he abides in equanimity, his mind becomes skilled21 in apprehending what is (in the ultimate sense) non-existent, because his mind has been diverted from apprehension of (what is existent in) the ultimate sense, namely, pleasure, (release from) pain, etc., owing to having no further concern such as “May beings be happy” or “May they be released from pain” or “May they not lose the success they have obtained.”

rows.pali: Athassa paramatthagāhato vimukhabhāvaparicitacittassa paramatthato avijjamānaggahaṇadukkhacittassa ca anukkamādhigataṃ viññāṇañcāyatanaṃ samatikkamma sabhāvato avijjamāne paramatthabhūtassa viññāṇassa abhāve cittaṃ upasaṃharato appakasireneva tattha cittaṃ pakkhandati.
rows.english: Now his mind has become used to being diverted from apprehension of [what is existent in] the ultimate sense, and his mind has become skilled in apprehending what is non-existent in the ultimate sense, (that is to say, living beings, which are a concept), and so when he surmounts the base consisting of boundless consciousness attained in due course and applies his mind to the absence, which is non-existent as to individual essence, of consciousness, which is a reality (is become—see M I 260) in the ultimate sense, then his mind enters into that (nothingness, that non-existence) without difficulty (see X.32).

rows.pali: Iti upekkhā ākiñcaññāyatanassa upanissayo hoti, na tato paraṃ, tasmā ākiñcaññāyatanaparamāti vuttāti.
rows.english: So equanimity is the basic support for the base consisting of nothingness, but not for what is beyond that. That is why it is called “having the base consisting of nothingness as the highest.”

rows.pali: 274.Evaṃ subhaparamādivasena etāsaṃ ānubhāvaṃ viditvā puna sabbāpetā dānādīnaṃ sabbakalyāṇadhammānaṃ paripūrikāti veditabbā.
rows.english: 124. When he has understood thus that the special efficacy of each resides respectively in “having beauty as the highest,” etc., he should besides understand how they bring to perfection all the good states beginning with giving.

rows.pali: Sattesu hi hitajjhāsayatāya sattānaṃ dukkhāsahanatāya, pattasampattivisesānaṃ ciraṭṭhitikāmatāya, sabbasattesu ca pakkhapātābhāvena samappavattacittā mahāsattā "imassa dātabbaṃ, imassa na dātabba"nti vibhāgaṃ akatvā sabbasattānaṃ sukhanidānaṃ dānaṃ denti.
rows.english: For the Great Beings’ minds retain their balance by giving preference to beings’ welfare, by dislike of beings’ suffering, by desire for the various successes achieved by beings to last, and by impartiality towards all beings. And to all beings they give gifts, which are a source a pleasure, without discriminating thus: “It must be given to this one; it must not be given to this one.”

rows.pali: Tesaṃ upaghātaṃ parivajjayantā sīlaṃ samādiyanti.
rows.english: And in order to avoid doing harm to beings they undertake the precepts of virtue.

rows.pali: Sīlaparipūraṇatthaṃ nekkhammaṃ bhajanti.
rows.english: They practice renunciation for the purpose of perfecting their virtue.

rows.pali: Sattānaṃ hitāhitesu asammohatthāya paññaṃ pariyodapenti.
rows.english: They cleanse their understanding for the purpose of non-confusion about what is good and bad for beings.

rows.pali: Sattānaṃ hitasukhatthāya niccaṃ vīriyamārabhanti.
rows.english: They constantly arouse energy, having beings’ welfare and happiness at heart.

rows.pali: Uttamavīriyavasena vīrabhāvaṃ pattāpi ca sattānaṃ nānappakārakaṃ aparādhaṃ khamanti.
rows.english: When they have acquired heroic fortitude through supreme energy, they become patient with beings’ many kinds of faults.

rows.pali: "Idaṃ vo dassāma karissāmā"ti kataṃ paṭiññaṃ na visaṃvādenti.
rows.english: They do not deceive when promising “We shall give you this; we shall do this for you.”

rows.pali: Tesaṃ hitasukhāya avicalādhiṭṭhānā honti.
rows.english: They are unshakably resolute upon beings’ welfare and happiness.

rows.pali: Tesu avicalāya mettāya pubbakārino honti.
rows.english: Through unshakable loving-kindness they place them first [before themselves].

rows.pali: Upekkhāya paccupakāraṃ nāsīsantīti evaṃ pāramiyo pūretvā yāva dasabalacatuvesārajjachaasādhāraṇañāṇaaṭṭhārasabuddhadhammappabhede sabbepi kalyāṇadhamme paripūrentīti evaṃ dānādisabbakalyāṇadhammaparipūrikā etāva hontīti.
rows.english: Through equanimity they expect no reward. Having thus fulfilled the [ten] perfections, these [divine abidings] then perfect all the good states classed as the ten powers, the four kinds of fearlessness, the six kinds of knowledge not shared [by disciples], and the eighteen states of the Enlightened One.22 This is how they bring to perfection all the good states beginning with giving.

rows.pali: 240.Anussatikammaṭṭhānānantaraṃ uddiṭṭhesu pana mettā, karuṇā, muditā, upekkhāti imesu catūsu brahmavihāresu mettaṃ bhāvetukāmena tāva ādikammikena yogāvacarena upacchinnapalibodhena gahitakammaṭṭhānena bhattakiccaṃ katvā bhattasammadaṃ paṭivinodetvā vivitte padese supaññatte āsane sukhanisinnena ādito tāva dose ādīnavo, khantiyañca ānisaṃso paccavekkhitabbo.
rows.english: 1.[295] The four divine abidings were mentioned next to the recollections as meditation subjects (III.105). They are loving-kindness, compassion, gladness and equanimity. A meditator, who wants to develop firstly loving-kindness among these, if he is a beginner, should sever the impediments and learn the meditation subject. Then, when he has done the work connected with the meal and got rid of any dizziness due to it, he should seat himself comfortably on a well-prepared seat in a secluded place. To start with, he should review the danger in hate and the advantage in patience.

rows.pali: Kasmā?
rows.english: 2. Why?

rows.pali: Imāya hi bhāvanāya doso pahātabbo, khanti adhigantabbā.
rows.english: Because hate has to be abandoned and patience attained in the development of this meditation subject,

rows.pali: Na ca sakkā kiñci adiṭṭhādīnavaṃ pahātuṃ, aviditānisaṃsaṃ vā adhigantuṃ.
rows.english: and he cannot abandon unseen dangers and attain unknown advantages.

rows.pali: Tasmā "duṭṭho kho, āvuso, dosena abhibhūto pariyādiṇṇacitto pāṇampi hanatī"tiādīnaṃ (a. ni. 3.72) vasena dose ādīnavo daṭṭhabbo.
rows.english: Now, the danger in hate should be seen in accordance with such suttas as this: “Friends, when a man hates, is a prey to hate and his mind is obsessed by hate, he kills living things, and …” (A I 216).

rows.pali: Athevaṃ diṭṭhādīnavato dosato cittaṃ vivecanatthāya, viditānisaṃsāya ca khantiyā saṃyojanatthāya mettābhāvanā ārabhitabbā.
rows.english: 3.Thereupon he should embark upon the development of loving-kindness for the purpose of secluding the mind from hate seen as a danger and introducing it to patience known as an advantage.

rows.pali: Ārabhantena ca āditova puggalabhedo jānitabbo "imesu puggalesu mettā paṭhamaṃ na bhāvetabbā, imesu neva bhāvetabbā"ti.
rows.english: But when he begins, he must know that some persons are of the wrong sort at the very beginning and that loving-kindness should be developed towards certain kinds of persons and not towards certain other kinds at first.

rows.pali: Ayañhi mettā appiyapuggale, atippiyasahāyake, majjhatte, verīpuggaleti imesu catūsu paṭhamaṃ na bhāvetabbā.
rows.english: 4.For loving-kindness should not be developed at first towards the following four kinds of persons: an antipathetic person, a very dearly loved friend, a neutral person, and a hostile person.

rows.pali: Liṅgavisabhāge odhiso na bhāvetabbā.
rows.english: Also it should not be developed specifically (see §49) towards the opposite sex,

rows.pali: Kālakate na bhāvetabbāva.
rows.english: or towards a dead person.

rows.pali: Kiṃkāraṇā appiyādīsu paṭhamaṃ na bhāvetabbā?
rows.english: 5. What is the reason why it should not be developed at first towards an antipathetic person and the others?

rows.pali: Appiyaṃ hi piyaṭṭhāne ṭhapento kilamati.
rows.english: To put an antipathetic person in a dear one’s place is fatiguing.

rows.pali: Atippiyasahāyakaṃ majjhattaṭṭhāne ṭhapento kilamati, appamattakepi cassa dukkhe uppanne ārodanākārappatto viya hoti.
rows.english: To put a very dearly loved friend in a neutral person’s place is fatiguing; and if the slightest mischance befalls the friend, he feels like weeping.

rows.pali: Majjhattaṃ garuṭṭhāne ca piyaṭṭhāne ca ṭhapento kilamati.
rows.english: To put a neutral person in a respected one’s or a dear one’s place is fatiguing.

rows.pali: Verimanussarato kodho uppajjati, tasmā appiyādīsu paṭhamaṃ na bhāvetabbā.
rows.english: Anger springs up in him if he recollects a hostile person. That is why it should not be developed at first towards an antipathetic person and the rest.

rows.pali: Liṅgavisabhāge pana tameva ārabbha odhiso bhāventassa rāgo uppajjati.
rows.english: 6.Then, if he develops it specifically towards the opposite sex, lust inspired by that person springs up in him.

rows.pali: Aññataro kira amaccaputto kulūpakattheraṃ pucchi "bhante, kassa mettā bhāvetabbā"ti?
rows.english: An elder supported by a family was asked, it seems, by a friend’s son, “Venerable sir, towards whom should loving-kindness be developed?”

rows.pali: Thero "piyapuggale"ti āha.
rows.english: The elder told him, “Towards a person one loves.”

rows.pali: Tassa attano bhariyā piyā hoti.
rows.english: He loved his own wife.

rows.pali: So tassā mettaṃ bhāvento sabbarattiṃ bhittiyuddhamakāsi.
rows.english: Through developing loving-kindness towards her he was fighting against the wall all the night.1

rows.pali: Tasmā liṅgavisabhāge odhiso na bhāvetabbā.
rows.english: That is why it should not be developed specifically towards the opposite sex.

rows.pali: Kālakate pana bhāvento neva appanaṃ, na upacāraṃ pāpuṇāti.
rows.english: 7.But if he develops it towards a dead person, he reaches neither absorption nor access.

rows.pali: Aññataro kira daharabhikkhu ācariyaṃ ārabbha mettaṃ ārabhi.
rows.english: A young bhikkhu, it seems, had started developing loving-kindness inspired by his teacher.

rows.pali: Tassa mettā nappavattati.
rows.english: His loving-kindness made no headway at all.

rows.pali: So mahātherassa santikaṃ gantvā "bhante, paguṇāva me mettājhānasamāpatti, na ca naṃ samāpajjituṃ sakkomi, kiṃ nu kho kāraṇa"nti āha.
rows.english: He went to a senior elder and told him, “Venerable sir, I am quite familiar with attaining jhāna through loving-kindness, and yet I cannot attain it. What is the matter?”

rows.pali: Thero "nimittaṃ, āvuso, gavesāhī"ti āha.
rows.english: The elder said, “Seek the sign, friend, [the object of your meditation].”

rows.pali: So gavesanto ācariyassa matabhāvaṃ ñatvā aññaṃ ārabbha mettāyanto samāpattiṃ appesi.
rows.english: He did so. Finding that his teacher had died, he proceeded with developing loving-kindness inspired by another and attained absorption.

rows.pali: Tasmā kālakate na bhāvetabbāva.
rows.english: That is why it should not be developed towards one who is dead.

rows.pali: 241.Sabbapaṭhamaṃ pana "ahaṃ sukhito homi niddukkho"ti vā, "avero abyāpajjo anīgho sukhī attānaṃ pariharāmī"ti vā evaṃ punappunaṃ attaniyeva bhāvetabbā.
rows.english: 8.First of all it should be developed only towards oneself, doing it repeatedly thus: “May I be happy and free from suffering” or “May I keep myself free from enmity, affliction and anxiety and live happily.”

rows.pali: Evaṃ sante yaṃ vibhaṅge (vibha. 643) vuttaṃ –
rows.english: 9.If that is so, does it not conflict with what is said in the texts? For there is no mention of any development of it towards oneself in what is said in the Vibhaṅga:

rows.pali: "Yañca paṭisambhidāyaṃ (paṭi. ma. 2.22) –
rows.english: and in the Paṭisambhidā:

rows.pali: Vuttaṃ.
rows.english: in what is said (above).

rows.pali: Yañca mettasutte (khu. pā. 9.3; su. ni. 145) –
rows.english: and in the Mettā Sutta:

rows.pali: Sacepi hi vassasataṃ vassasahassaṃ vā "ahaṃ sukhito homī"tiādinā nayena attani mettaṃ bhāveti, nevassa appanā uppajjati.
rows.english: For even if he developed loving-kindness for a hundred or a thousand years in this way, “I am happy” and so on, absorption would never arise.

rows.pali: "Ahaṃ sukhito homī"ti bhāvayato pana yathā ahaṃ sukhakāmo dukkhapaṭikkūlo jīvitukāmo amaritukāmo ca, evaṃ aññepi sattāti attānaṃ sakkhiṃ katvā aññasattesu hitasukhakāmatā uppajjati.
rows.english: But if he develops it in this way: “I am happy. Just as I want to be happy and dread pain, as I want to live and not to die, so do other beings, too,” making himself the example, then desire for other beings’ welfare and happiness arises in him.

rows.pali: Bhagavatāpi –
rows.english: And by the Blessed One’s saying:

rows.pali: Tasmā sakkhibhāvatthaṃ paṭhamaṃ attānaṃ mettāya pharitvā tadanantaraṃ sukhappavattanatthaṃ yvāyaṃ piyo manāpo garu bhāvanīyo ācariyo vā ācariyamatto vā upajjhāyo vā upajjhāyamatto vā tassa dānapiyavacanādīni piyamanāpattakāraṇāni sīlasutādīni garubhāvanīyattakāraṇāni ca anussaritvā "esa sappuriso sukhī hotu niddukkho"tiādinā nayena mettā bhāvetabbā.
rows.english: 11.So he should first, as example, pervade himself with loving-kindness. Next after that, in order to proceed easily, he can recollect such gifts,2 kind words, etc., as inspire love and endearment, such virtue, learning, etc., as inspire respect and reverence met with in a teacher or his equivalent or a preceptor or his equivalent, developing loving-kindness towards him in the way beginning, “May this good man be happy and free from suffering.”

rows.pali: Evarūpe ca puggale kāmaṃ appanā sampajjati, iminā pana bhikkhunā tāvatakeneva tuṭṭhiṃ anāpajjitvā sīmāsambhedaṃ kattukāmena tadanantaraṃ atippiyasahāyake, atippiyasahāyakato majjhatte, majjhattato verīpuggale mettā bhāvetabbā.
rows.english: With such a person, of course, he attains absorption. 12.But if this bhikkhu does not rest content with just that much and wants to break down the barriers, he should next, after that, develop loving-kindness towards a very dearly loved friend, then towards a neutral person as a very dearly loved friend, then towards a hostile person as neutral.

rows.pali: Bhāventena ca ekekasmiṃ koṭṭhāse muduṃ kammaniyaṃ cittaṃ katvā tadanantare tadanantare upasaṃharitabbaṃ.
rows.english: And while he does so, he should make his mind malleable and wieldy in each instance before passing on to the next.

rows.pali: Yassa pana verīpuggalo vā natthi, mahāpurisajātikattā vā anatthaṃ karontepi pare verīsaññāva nuppajjati, tena "majjhatte me mettacittaṃ kammaniyaṃ jātaṃ, idāni naṃ verimhi upasaṃharāmī"ti byāpārova na kātabbo.
rows.english: 13.But if he has no enemy, or he is of the type of a great man who does not perceive another as an enemy even when the other does him harm, he should not interest himself as follows: “Now that my consciousness of loving-kindness has become wieldy towards a neutral person, I shall apply it to a hostile one.” [298]

rows.pali: Yassa pana atthi, taṃ sandhāya vuttaṃ "majjhattato verīpuggale mettā bhāvetabbā"ti.
rows.english: Rather it was about one who actually has an enemy that it was said above that he should develop loving-kindness towards a hostile person as neutral.

rows.pali: 243.Sace panassa verimhi cittamupasaṃharato tena katāparādhānussaraṇena paṭighamuppajjati, athānena purimapuggalesu yattha katthaci punappunaṃ mettaṃ samāpajjitvā vuṭṭhahitvā punappunaṃ taṃ puggalaṃ mettāyantena paṭighaṃ vinodetabbaṃ.
rows.english: 14.If resentment arises in him when he applies his mind to a hostile person because he remembers wrongs done by that person, he should get rid of the resentment by entering repeatedly into loving-kindness [jhāna] towards any of the first-mentioned persons and then, after he has emerged each time, directing loving-kindness towards that person.

rows.pali: Sace evampi vāyamato na nibbāti, atha –
rows.english: 15.But if it does not die out in spite of his efforts, then:

rows.pali: Tañca kho iminā ākārena attānaṃ ovadanteneva "are kujjhanapurisa, nanu vuttaṃ bhagavatā –
rows.english: He should admonish himself in this way: “Now, you who get angry, has not the Blessed One said this:

rows.pali: "So dāni tvaṃ evaṃ kujjhanto na ceva bhagavato sāsanakaro bhavissasi, paṭikujjhanto ca kuddhapurisatopi pāpiyo hutvā na dujjayaṃ saṅgāmaṃ jessasi, sapattakaraṇe ca dhamme attāva attano karissasi, chavālātūpamo ca bhavissasī"ti.
rows.english: “If you are angry now, you will be one who does not carry out the Blessed One’s teaching; by repaying an angry man in kind you will be worse than the angry man and not win the battle hard to win; you will yourself do to yourself the things that help your enemy; and you will be like a pyre log. ” (Source untraced)

rows.pali: 244.Tassevaṃ ghaṭayato vāyamato sace taṃ paṭighaṃ vūpasammati, iccetaṃ kusalaṃ.
rows.english: 16.If his resentment subsides when he strives and makes effort in this way, it is good.

rows.pali: No ce vūpasammati, atha yo yo dhammo tassa puggalassa vūpasanto hoti parisuddho, anussariyamāno pasādaṃ āvahati, taṃ taṃ anussaritvā āghāto paṭivinetabbo.
rows.english: If not, then he should remove irritation by remembering some controlled and purified state in that person, which inspires confidence when remembered.

rows.pali: Ekaccassa hi kāyasamācārova upasanto hoti.
rows.english: 17.For one person may be controlled in his bodily behaviour

rows.pali: Upasantabhāvo cassa bahuṃ vattapaṭipattiṃ karontassa sabbajanena ñāyati.
rows.english: with his control in doing an extensive course of duty known to all,

rows.pali: Vacīsamācāramanosamācārā pana avūpasantā honti.
rows.english: though his verbal and mental behaviour are not controlled.

rows.pali: Tassa te acintetvā kāyasamācāravūpasamoyeva anussaritabbo.
rows.english: Then the latter should be ignored and the control in his bodily behaviour remembered.

rows.pali: Ekaccassa vacīsamācārova upasanto hoti.
rows.english: 18.Another may be controlled in his verbal behaviour,

rows.pali: Upasantabhāvo cassa sabbajanena ñāyati.
rows.english: and his control known to all—

rows.pali: So hi pakatiyā ca paṭisanthārakusalo hoti sakhilo sukhasambhāso sammodako uttānamukho pubbabhāsī madhurena sarena dhammaṃ osāreti, parimaṇḍalehi padabyañjanehi dhammakathaṃ katheti.
rows.english: he may naturally be clever at welcoming kindly, easy to talk with, congenial, open-countenanced, deferential in speech, and he may expound the Dhamma with a sweet voice and give explanations of Dhamma with well- rounded phrases and details—

rows.pali: Kāyasamācāramanosamācārā pana avūpasantā honti, tassa te acintetvā vacīsamācāravūpasamoyeva anussaritabbo.
rows.english: though his bodily and mental behaviour are not controlled. Then the latter should be ignored and the control in his verbal behaviour remembered.

rows.pali: Ekaccassa manosamācārova upasanto hoti, upasantabhāvo cassa cetiyavandanādīsu sabbajanassa pākaṭo hoti.
rows.english: 19. Another may be controlled in his mental behaviour, and his control in worshiping at shrines, etc., evident to all.

rows.pali: Yo hi avūpasantacitto hoti, so cetiyaṃ vā bodhiṃ vā there vā vandamāno na sakkaccaṃ vandati, dhammassavanamaṇḍape vikkhittacitto vā pacalāyanto vā nisīdati.
rows.english: For when one who is uncontrolled in mind pays homage at a shrine or at an Enlightenment Tree or to elders, he does not do it carefully, [300] and he sits in the Dhamma-preaching pavilion with mind astray or nodding,

rows.pali: Upasantacitto pana okappetvā vandati, ohitasoto aṭṭhiṃkatvā kāyena vā vācāya vā cittappasādaṃ karonto dhammaṃ suṇāti.
rows.english: while one whose mind is controlled pays homage carefully and deliberately, listens to the Dhamma attentively, remembering it, and evincing the confidence in his mind through his body or his speech.

rows.pali: Iti ekaccassa manosamācārova upasanto hoti, kāyavacīsamācārā avūpasantā honti, tassa te acintetvā manosamācāravūpasamoyeva anussaritabbo.
rows.english: So another may be only controlled in his mental behaviour, though his bodily and verbal behaviour are not controlled. Then the latter should be ignored and the control in his mental behaviour remembered.

rows.pali: Ekaccassa pana imesu tīsu dhammesu ekopi avūpasanto hoti, tasmiṃ puggale "kiñcāpi esa idāni manussaloke carati, atha kho katipāhassa accayena aṭṭhamahānirayasoḷasaussadanirayaparipūrako bhavissatī"ti kāruññaṃ upaṭṭhapetabbaṃ.
rows.english: 20.But there may be another in whom not even one of these three things is controlled. Then compassion for that person should be aroused thus: “Though he is going about in the human world now, nevertheless after a certain number of days he will find himself in [one of] the eight great hells or the sixteen prominent hells.4”

rows.pali: Kāruññampi hi paṭicca āghāto vūpasammati.
rows.english: For irritation subsides too through compassion.

rows.pali: Ekaccassa tayopime dhammā vūpasantā honti, tassa yaṃ yaṃ icchati, taṃ taṃ anussaritabbaṃ.
rows.english: In yet another all three may be controlled. Then he can remember any of the three in that person, whichever he likes;

rows.pali: Tādise hi puggale na dukkarā hoti mettābhāvanāti.
rows.english: for the development of loving-kindness towards such a person is easy.

rows.pali: Imassa ca atthassa āvibhāvatthaṃ – "pañcime, āvuso, āghātapaṭivinayā.
rows.english: 21.And in order to make the meaning of this clear “Bhikkhus, there are five ways of dispelling annoyance

rows.pali: Yattha bhikkhuno uppanno āghāto sabbaso paṭivinodetabbo"ti (a. ni. 5.162) idaṃ pañcakanipāte āghātapaṭivinayasuttaṃ vitthāretabbaṃ.
rows.english: whereby annoyance arisen in a bhikkhu can be entirely dispelled” (A III 186–90) - this following sutta from the Book of Fives should be cited in full.

rows.pali: 245.Sace panassa evampi vāyamato āghāto uppajjatiyeva, athānena evaṃ attā ovaditabbo –
rows.english: 22. But if irritation still arises in him in spite of his efforts, then he should admonish himself thus:

rows.pali: 246.Sace panassa evaṃ attānaṃ ovadatopi paṭighaṃ neva vūpasammati, athānena attano ca parassa ca kammassakatā paccavekkhitabbā.
rows.english: 23.But if resentment does not subside when he admonishes himself thus, then he should review the fact that he himself and the other are owners of their deeds (kamma).

rows.pali: Tattha attano tāva evaṃ paccavekkhitabbā "ambho tvaṃ tassa kuddho kiṃ karissasi?
rows.english: Herein, he should first review this in himself thus: “Now, what is the point of your getting angry with him?

rows.pali: Nanu taveva cetaṃ dosanidānaṃ kammaṃ anatthāya saṃvattissati?
rows.english: Will not this kamma of yours that has anger as its source lead to your own harm?

rows.pali: Kammassako hi tvaṃ kammadāyādo kammayoni kammabandhu kammapaṭisaraṇo, yaṃ kammaṃ karissasi, tassa dāyādo bhavissasi, idañca te kammaṃ neva sammāsambodhiṃ, na paccekabodhiṃ, na sāvakabhūmiṃ, na brahmattasakkattacakkavattipadesarājādisampattīnaṃ aññataraṃ sampattiṃ sādhetuṃ samatthaṃ, atha kho sāsanato cāvetvā vighāsādādibhāvassa ceva nerayikādidukkhavisesānañca te saṃvattanikamidaṃ kammaṃ.
rows.english: For you are the owner of your deeds, heir of your deeds, having deeds as your parent, deeds as your kin, deeds as your refuge; you will become the heir of whatever deeds you do (see A III 186). And this is not the kind of deed to bring you to full enlightenment, to undeclared enlightenment or to the disciple’s grade, or to any such position as the status of Brahmā or Sakka, or the throne of a Wheel-turning Monarch or a regional king, etc.; but rather this is the kind of deed to lead to your fall from the Dispensation, even to the status of the eaters of scraps, etc., and to the manifold suffering in the hells, and so on.

rows.pali: So tvaṃ idaṃ karonto ubhohi hatthehi vītaccite vā aṅgāre, gūthaṃ vā gahetvā paraṃ paharitukāmo puriso viya attānameva paṭhamaṃ dahasi ceva duggandhañca karosī"ti.
rows.english: By doing this you are like a man who wants to hit another and picks up a burning ember or excrement in his hand and so first burns himself or makes himself stink.”

rows.pali: Evaṃ attano kammassakataṃ paccavekkhitvā parassapi evaṃ paccavekkhitabbā "esopi tava kujjhitvā kiṃ karissati?
rows.english: 24. Having reviewed ownership of deeds in himself in this way, he should review it in the other also: “And what is the point of his getting angry with you?

rows.pali: Nanu etassevetaṃ anatthāya saṃvattissati?
rows.english: Will it not lead to his own harm?

rows.pali: Kammassako hi ayamāyasmā kammadāyādo - pe - yaṃ kammaṃ karissati, tassa dāyādo bhavissati.
rows.english: For that venerable one is owner of his deeds, heir of his deeds … he will become the heir of whatever deeds he does.

rows.pali: Idañcassa kammaṃ neva sammāsambodhiṃ, na paccekabodhiṃ, na sāvakabhūmiṃ, na brahmattasakkattacakkavattipadesarājādisampattīnaṃ aññataraṃ sampattiṃ sādhetuṃ samatthaṃ, atha kho sāsanato cāvetvā vighāsādādibhāvassa ceva nerayikādidukkhavisesānañcassa saṃvattanikamidaṃ kammaṃ.
rows.english: And this is not the kind of deed to bring him to full enlightenment, to undeclared enlightenment or to the disciple’s grade, or to any such position as the status of Brahmā or Sakka, or to the throne of a Wheel-turning Monarch or a regional king, etc.; but rather this is the kind of deed to lead to his fall from the Dispensation, even to the status of the eaters of scraps, etc., and to the manifold suffering in the hells, and so on.

rows.pali: Svāyaṃ idaṃ karonto paṭivāte ṭhatvā paraṃ rajena okiritukāmo puriso viya attānaṃyeva okirati.
rows.english: By doing this he is like a man who wants to throw dust at another against the wind and only covers himself with it.”

rows.pali: Vuttañhetaṃ bhagavatā –
rows.english: For this is said by the Blessed One:

rows.pali: 247.Sace panassa evaṃ kammassakatampi paccavekkhato neva vūpasammati, athānena satthu pubbacariyaguṇā anussaritabbā.
rows.english: 25.But if it still does not subside in him when he reviews ownership of deeds in this way, then he should review the special qualities of the Master’s former conduct.

rows.pali: Tatrāyaṃ paccavekkhaṇānayo – ambho pabbajita, nanu te satthā pubbeva sambodhā anabhisambuddho bodhisattopi samāno cattāri asaṅkhyeyyāni kappasatasahassañca pāramiyo pūrayamāno tattha tattha vadhakesupi paccatthikesu cittaṃ nappadūsesi.
rows.english: 26.Here is the way of reviewing it: “Now you who have gone forth, is it not a fact that when your Master was a Bodhisatta before discovering full enlightenment, while he was still engaged in fulfilling the perfections during the four incalculable ages and a hundred thousand aeons, he did not allow hate to corrupt his mind even when his enemies tried to murder him on various occasions?

rows.pali: Seyyathidaṃ, sīlavajātake tāva attano deviyā paduṭṭhena pāpaamaccena ānītassa paṭirañño tiyojanasataṃ rajjaṃ gaṇhantassa nisedhanatthāya uṭṭhitānaṃ amaccānaṃ āvudhampi chupituṃ na adāsi.
rows.english: 27.“For example, in the Sīlavant Birth Story (J-a I 261) when his friends rose to prevent his kingdom of three hundred leagues being seized by an enemy king who had been incited by a wicked minister in whose mind his own queen had sown hate for him, he did not allow them to lift a weapon.

rows.pali: Puna saddhiṃ amaccasahassena āmakasusāne galappamāṇaṃ bhūmiṃ khaṇitvā nikhaññamāno cittappadosamattampi akatvā kuṇapakhādanatthaṃ āgatānaṃ siṅgālānaṃ paṃsuviyūhanaṃ nissāya purisakāraṃ katvā paṭiladdhajīvito yakkhānubhāvena attano sirigabbhaṃ oruyha sirisayane sayitaṃ paccatthikaṃ disvā kopaṃ akatvāva aññamaññaṃ sapathaṃ katvā taṃ mittaṭṭhāne ṭhapayitvā āha –
rows.english: Again when he was buried, along with a thousand companions, up to the neck in a hole dug in the earth in a charnel ground, he had no thought of hate. And when, after saving his life by a heroic effort helped by jackals scraping away soil when they had come to devour the corpses, he went with the aid of a spirit to his own bedroom and saw his enemy lying on his own bed, he was not angry but treated him as a friend, undertaking a mutual pledge, and he then exclaimed:

rows.pali: Khantivādījātake dummedhena kāsiraññā "kiṃvādī tvaṃ samaṇā"ti puṭṭho "khantivādī nāmāha"nti vutte sakaṇṭakāhi kasāhi tāḷetvā hatthapādesu chijjamānesu kopamattampi nākāsi.
rows.english: 28. “And in the Khantivādin Birth Story he was asked by the stupid king of Kāsi (Benares), ‘What do you preach, monk? ’ and he replied, ‘I am a preacher of patience’; and when the king had him flogged with scourges of thorns and had his hands and feet cut off, he felt not the slightest anger (see J-a III 39).

rows.pali: Anacchariyañcetaṃ, yaṃ mahallako pabbajjūpagato evaṃ kareyya.
rows.english: 29.“It is perhaps not so wonderful that an adult who had actually gone forth into homelessness should have acted in that way;

rows.pali: Cūḷadhammapālajātake pana uttānaseyyakopi samāno –
rows.english: but also as an infant he did so. For in the Cūḷa-Dhammapāla Birth Story:

rows.pali: Evaṃ vippalapamānāya mātuyā pitarā mahāpatāpena nāma raññā vaṃsakaḷīresu viya catūsu hatthapādesu chedāpitesu tāvatāpi santuṭṭhiṃ anāpajjitvā sīsamassa chindathāti āṇatte "ayaṃ dāni te cittapariggaṇhanakālo, idāni ambho dhammapāla, sīsacchedāṇāpake pitari, sīsacchedake purise, paridevamānāya mātari, attani cāti imesu catūsu samacitto hohī"ti daḷhaṃ samādānamadhiṭṭhāya paduṭṭhākāramattampi nākāsi.
rows.english: his hands and feet were ordered to be lopped off like four bamboo shoots by his father, King Mahāpatāpa, and his mother lamented over him thus (as above). Then his father, still not satisfied, commanded that his head be cut off as well. But even then he had not the least trace of hate, since he had firmly resolved thus: ‘Now is the time to restrain your mind; now, good Dhammapāla, be impartial towards these four persons, that is to say, towards your father who is having your head cut off, the man who is beheading you, your lamenting mother, and yourself.’

rows.pali: Idañcāpi anacchariyameva, yaṃ manussabhūto evamakāsi.
rows.english: 30.“And it is perhaps not so wonderful that one who had become a human being should have acted in that way;

rows.pali: Tiracchānabhūtopi pana chaddanto nāma vāraṇo hutvā visappitena sallena nābhiyaṃ viddhopi tāva anatthakārimhi luddake cittaṃ nappadūsesi.
rows.english: but also as an animal he did so. For while the Bodhisatta was the elephant called Chaddanta he was pierced in the navel by a poisoned shaft. But even then he allowed no hate towards the hunter who had wounded him to corrupt his mind,

rows.pali: Yathāha –
rows.english: according as it is said:

rows.pali: Evaṃ vatvā ca kāsirañño mahesiyā tava dantānamatthāya pesitomhi bhadanteti vutte tassā manorathaṃ pūrento chabbaṇṇarasminiccharaṇasamujjalitacārusobhe attano dante chetvā adāsi.
rows.english: “And when the elephant had spoken thus and was told, ‘Sir, I have been sent by the king of Kāsi’s queen to get your tusks,’ in order to fulfil her wish he cut off his own tusks whose gorgeous radiance glittered with the flashes of the six- coloured rays and gave them to him.

rows.pali: Mahākapi hutvā attanāyeva pabbatapapātato uddharitena purisena –
rows.english: 31.“And when he was the Great Monkey, the man whom he had pulled out of a rocky chasm thought:

rows.pali: Bhūridatto nāma nāgarājā hutvā uposathaṅgāni adhiṭṭhāya vammikamuddhani sayamāno kappuṭṭhānaggisadisena osadhena sakalasarīre siñciyamānopi peḷāya pakkhipitvā sakalajambudīpe kīḷāpiyamānopi tasmiṃ brāhmaṇe manopadosamattampi na akāsi.
rows.english: 32.“And while he was the royal nāga (serpent) Bhūridatta, [304] when he had undertaken the Uposatha precepts and was lying on the top of a termite-mound, though he was [caught and] sprinkled with medicinal charms resembling the fire that ushers in the end of an aeon, and was put into a box and treated as a plaything throughout the whole of Jambudīpa, yet he had no trace of hate for that brahman,

rows.pali: Yathāha –
rows.english: according as it is said:

rows.pali: Campeyyopināgarājā hutvā ahituṇḍikena viheṭhiyamāno manopadosamattampi nuppādesi.
rows.english: 33.“And when he was the royal nāga Campeyya he let no hate spring up in his mind while he was being cruelly treated by a snake charmer,

rows.pali: Yathāha –
rows.english: according as it is said:

rows.pali: Saṅkhapālanāgarājā hutvā tikhiṇāhi sattīhi aṭṭhasu ṭhānesu ovijjhitvā pahāramukhehi sakaṇṭakā latāyo pavesetvā nāsāya daḷhaṃ rajjuṃ pakkhipitvā soḷasahi bhojaputtehi kājenādāya vayhamāno dharaṇītale ghaṃsiyamānasarīro mahantaṃ dukkhaṃ paccanubhonto kujjhitvā olokitamatteneva sabbe bhojaputte bhasmaṃ kātuṃ samatthopi samāno cakkhuṃ ummīletvā paduṭṭhākāramattampi na akāsi.
rows.english: 34.“And when he was the royal nāga Saṅkhapāla, while he was being carried along on a carrying pole by the sixteen village boys after they had wounded him in eight places with sharp spears and inserted thorn creepers into the wounds’ orifices, and while, after threading a strong rope through his nose, they were causing him great agony by dragging him along bumping his body on the surface of the ground, though he was capable of turning those village boys to cinders with a mere glance, yet he did not even show the least trace of hate on opening his eyes,

rows.pali: Yathāha –
rows.english: according as it is said:

rows.pali: Na kevalañca etāneva, aññānipi mātuposakajātakādīsu anekāni acchariyāni akāsi.
rows.english: 35.“And he performed not only these wonders but also many others too such as those told in the Mātuposaka Birth Story (J-a IV 90).

rows.pali: Tassa te idāni sabbaññutaṃ pattaṃ sadevaloke kenaci appaṭisamakhantiguṇaṃ taṃ bhagavantaṃ satthāraṃ apadisato paṭighacittaṃ nāma uppādetuṃ ativiya ayuttaṃ appatirūpanti.
rows.english: Now, it is in the highest degree improper and unbecoming to you to arouse thoughts of resentment, since you are emulating as your Master that Blessed One who reached omniscience and who has in the special quality of patience no equal in the world with its deities. ”

rows.pali: 248.Sace panassa evaṃ satthu pubbacaritaguṇaṃ paccavekkhatopi dīgharattaṃ kilesānaṃ dāsabyaṃ upagatassa neva taṃ paṭighaṃ vūpasammati, athānena anamataggiyāni paccavekkhitabbāni.
rows.english: 36.But if, as he reviews the special qualities of the Master’s former conduct, the resentment still does not subside in him, since he has long been used to the slavery of defilement, then he should review the suttas that deal with the beginninglessness [of the round of rebirths].

rows.pali: Tatra hi vuttaṃ –
rows.english: Here is what is said:

rows.pali: Tasmā tasmiṃ puggale evaṃ cittaṃ uppādetabbaṃ, "ayaṃ kira me atīte mātā hutvā dasamāse kucchiyā pariharitvā muttakarīsakheḷasiṅghāṇikādīni haricandanaṃ viya ajigucchamānā apanetvā ure naccāpentī aṅgena pariharamānā posesi, pitā hutvā ajapathasaṅkupathādīni gantvā vāṇijjaṃ payojayamāno mayhamatthāya jīvitampi pariccajitvā ubhatobyūḷhe saṅgāme pavisitvā nāvāya mahāsamuddaṃ pakkhanditvā aññāni ca dukkarāni karitvā 'puttake posessāmī'ti tehi tehi upāyehi dhanaṃ saṃharitvā maṃ posesi.
rows.english: Consequently he should think about that person thus: “This person, it seems, as my mother in the past carried me in her womb for ten months and removed from me without disgust, as if it were yellow sandalwood, my urine, excrement, spittle, snot, etc., and played with me in her lap, and nourished me, carrying me about on her hip. And this person as my father went by goat paths and paths set on piles,5 etc., to pursue the trade of merchant, and he risked his life for me by going into battle in double array, by sailing on the great ocean in ships and doing other difficult things, and he nourished me by bringing back wealth by one means or another thinking to feed his children.

rows.pali: Bhātā, bhaginī, putto, dhītā ca hutvāpi idañcidañcupakāraṃ akāsīti tatra me nappatirūpaṃ manaṃ padūsetu"nti.
rows.english: And as my brother, sister, son, daughter, this person gave me such and such help. So it is unbecoming for me to harbour hate for him in my mind. ”

rows.pali: 249.Sace pana evampi cittaṃ nibbāpetuṃ na sakkotiyeva, athānena evaṃ mettānisaṃsā paccavekkhitabbā – "ambho pabbajita, nanu vuttaṃ bhagavatā –
rows.english: 37.But if he is still unable to quench that thought in this way, then he should review the advantages of loving-kindness thus: “Now, you who have gone forth into homelessness, has it not been said by the Blessed One as follows:

rows.pali: "Sace tvaṃ idaṃ cittaṃ na nibbāpessasi, imehi ānisaṃsehi paribāhiro bhavissasī"ti.
rows.english: [306] If you do not stop this thought, you will be denied these advantages. ”

rows.pali: 250.Evampi nibbāpetuṃ asakkontena pana dhātuvinibbhogo kātabbo.
rows.english: 38.But if he is still unable to stop it in this way, he should try resolution into elements.

rows.pali: Kathaṃ?
rows.english: How?

rows.pali: "Ambho pabbajita, tvaṃ etassa kujjhamāno kassa kujjhasi?
rows.english: “Now, you who have gone forth into homelessness, when you are angry with him, what is it you are angry with?

rows.pali: Kiṃ kesānaṃ kujjhasi, udāhu lomānaṃ, nakhānaṃ - pe - muttassa kujjhasi?
rows.english: Is it head hairs you are angry with? Or body hairs? Or nails? … Or is it urine you are angry with?

rows.pali: Atha vā pana kesādīsu pathavīdhātuyā kujjhasi, āpodhātuyā, tejodhātuyā, vāyodhātuyā kujjhasi?
rows.english: Or alternatively, is it the earth element in the head hairs, etc., you are angry with? Or the water element? Or the fire element? Or is it the air element you are angry with?

rows.pali: Ye vā pañcakkhandhe dvādasāyatanāni aṭṭhārasa dhātuyo upādāya ayamāyasmā itthannāmoti vuccati, tesu kiṃ rūpakkhandhassa kujjhasi, udāhu vedanā saññā saṅkhāraviññāṇakkhandhassa kujjhasi?
rows.english: Or among the five aggregates or the twelve bases or the eighteen elements with respect to which this venerable one is called by such and such a name, which then, is it the materiality aggregate you are angry with? Or the feeling aggregate, the perception aggregate, the formations aggregate, the consciousness aggregate you are angry with?

rows.pali: Kiṃ vā cakkhāyatanassa kujjhasi, kiṃ rūpāyatanassa kujjhasi - pe - kiṃ manāyatanassa kujjhasi, kiṃ dhammāyatanassa kujjhasi?
rows.english: Or is it the eye base you are angry with? Or the visible-object base you are angry with? … Or the mind base you are angry with? Or the mental-object base you are angry with?

rows.pali: Kiṃ vā cakkhudhātuyā kujjhasi, kiṃ rūpadhātuyā, kiṃ cakkhuviññāṇadhātuyā - pe - kiṃ manodhātuyā, kiṃ dhammadhātuyā, kiṃ manoviññāṇadhātuyā"ti?
rows.english: Or is it the eye element you are angry with? Or the visible-object element? Or the eye-consciousness element? … Or the mind element? Or the mental-object element? Or the mind-consciousness element you are angry with?”

rows.pali: Evañhi dhātuvinibbhogaṃ karoto āragge sāsapassa viya ākāse cittakammassa viya ca kodhassa patiṭṭhānaṭṭhānaṃ na hoti.
rows.english: For when he tries the resolution into elements, his anger finds no foothold, like a mustard seed on the point of an awl or a painting on the air.

rows.pali: 251.Dhātuvinibbhogaṃ pana kātuṃ asakkontena dānasaṃvibhāgo kātabbo.
rows.english: 39.But if he cannot effect the resolution into elements, he should try the giving of a gift.

rows.pali: Attano santakaṃ parassa dātabbaṃ, parassa santakaṃ attanā gahetabbaṃ.
rows.english: It can either be given by himself to the other or accepted by himself from the other.

rows.pali: Sace pana paro bhinnājīvo hoti aparibhogārahaparikkhāro, attano santakameva dātabbaṃ.
rows.english: But if the other’s livelihood is not purified and his requisites are not proper to be used, it should be given by oneself.

rows.pali: Tassevaṃ karoto ekanteneva tasmiṃ puggale āghāto vūpasammati.
rows.english: And in the one who does this the annoyance with that person entirely subsides.

rows.pali: Itarassa ca atītajātito paṭṭhāya anubandhopi kodho taṅkhaṇaññeva vūpasammati, cittalapabbatavihāre tikkhattuṃ vuṭṭhāpitasenāsanena piṇḍapātikattherena "ayaṃ, bhante, aṭṭhakahāpaṇagghanako patto mama mātarā upāsikāya dinno dhammiyalābho, mahāupāsikāya puññalābhaṃ karothā"ti vatvā dinnaṃ pattaṃ laddhamahātherassa viya.
