rows.english: And in the other even anger that has been dogging him from a past birth subsides at the moment, as happened to the senior elder who received a bowl given to him at the Cittalapabbata Monastery by an almsfood-eater elder who had been three times made to move from his lodging by him, and who presented it with these words: “Venerable sir, this bowl worth eight ducats was given me by my mother who is a lay devotee, and it is rightly obtained; let the good lay devotee acquire merit.”

rows.pali: Evaṃ mahānubhāvametaṃ dānaṃ nāma.
rows.english: So efficacious is this act of giving.

rows.pali: Vuttampi cetaṃ –
rows.english: And this is said:

rows.pali: 252.Tassevaṃ verīpuggale vūpasantapaṭighassa yathā piyātippiyasahāyakamajjhattesu, evaṃ tasmimpi mettāvasena cittaṃ pavattati.
rows.english: 40. When his resentment towards that hostile person has been thus allayed, then he can turn his mind with loving-kindness towards that person too, just as towards the one who is dear, the very dear friend, and the neutral person.

rows.pali: Athānena punappunaṃ mettāyantena attani piyapuggale majjhatte verīpuggaleti catūsu janesu samacittataṃ sampādentena sīmāsambhedo kātabbo.
rows.english: Then he should break down the barriers by practicing loving-kindness over and over again, accomplishing mental impartiality towards the four persons, that is to say, himself, the dear person, the neutral person and the hostile person.

rows.pali: Tassidaṃ lakkhaṇaṃ, sace imasmiṃ puggale piyamajjhattaverīhi saddhiṃ attacatutthe ekasmiṃ padese nisinne corā āgantvā "bhante, ekaṃ bhikkhuṃ amhākaṃ dethā"ti vatvā "kiṃ kāraṇā"ti vutte "taṃ māretvā galalohitaṃ gahetvā balikaraṇatthāyā"ti vadeyyuṃ, tatra ceso bhikkhu "asukaṃ vā asukaṃ vā gaṇhantū"ti cinteyya, akatova hoti sīmāsambhedo.
rows.english: 41.The characteristic of it is this. Suppose this person is sitting in a place with a dear, a neutral, and a hostile person, himself being the fourth; then bandits come to him and say, “Venerable sir, give us a bhikkhu,” and on being asked why, they answer, “So that we may kill him and use the blood of his throat as an offering;” then if that bhikkhu thinks, “Let them take this one, or this one,” he has not broken down the barriers.

rows.pali: Sacepi "maṃ gaṇhantu, mā ime tayo"tipi cinteyya, akatova hoti sīmāsambhedo.
rows.english: And also if he thinks, “Let them take me but not these three,” he has not broken down the barriers either.

rows.pali: Kasmā?
rows.english: Why?

rows.pali: Yassa yassa hi gahaṇamicchati, tassa tassa ahitesī hoti, itaresaṃyeva hitesī hoti.
rows.english: Because he seeks the harm of him whom he wishes to be taken and seeks the welfare of the other only.

rows.pali: Yadā pana catunnaṃ janānamantare ekampi corānaṃ dātabbaṃ na passati, attani ca tesu ca tīsu janesu samameva cittaṃ pavatteti, kato hoti sīmāsambhedo.
rows.english: But it is when he does not see a single one among the four people to be given to the bandits and he directs his mind impartially towards himself and towards those three people that he has broken down the barriers.

rows.pali: Tenāhu porāṇā –
rows.english: Hence the Ancients said:

rows.pali: 253.Evaṃ sīmāsambhedasamakālameva ca iminā bhikkhunā nimittañca upacārañca laddhaṃ hoti.
rows.english: 43. Thus the sign and access are obtained by this bhikkhu simultaneously with the breaking down of the barriers.

rows.pali: Sīmāsambhede pana kate tameva nimittaṃ āsevanto bhāvento bahulīkaronto appakasireneva pathavīkasiṇe vuttanayeneva appanaṃ pāpuṇāti.
rows.english: But when breaking down of the barriers has been effected, he reaches absorption in the way described under the earth kasiṇa without trouble by cultivating, developing, and repeatedly practicing that same sign.

rows.pali: Ettāvatānena adhigataṃ hoti pañcaṅgavippahīnaṃ pañcaṅgasamannāgataṃ tividhakalyāṇaṃ dasalakkhaṇasampannaṃ paṭhamajjhānaṃ mettāsahagataṃ.
rows.english: At this point he has attained the first jhāna, which abandons five factors, possesses five factors, is good in three ways, is endowed with ten characteristics, and is accompanied by loving-kindness.

rows.pali: Adhigate ca tasmiṃ tadeva nimittaṃ āsevanto bhāvento bahulīkaronto anupubbena catukkanaye dutiyatatiyajjhānāni, pañcakanaye dutiyatatiyacatutthajjhānāni ca pāpuṇāti.
rows.english: And when that has been obtained, then by cultivating, developing, and repeatedly practicing that same sign, he successively reaches the second and third jhānas in the fourfold system, and the second, third and fourth in the fivefold system. [308]

rows.pali: So hi paṭhamajjhānādīnaṃ aññataravasena mettāsahagatena cetasā ekaṃ disaṃ pharitvā viharati.
rows.english: 44.Now, it is by means of one of these jhānas beginning with the first that he “Dwells pervading (intent upon) one direction with his heart endued with loving- kindness,

rows.pali: Tathā dutiyaṃ, tathā tatiyaṃ, tathā catutthaṃ.
rows.english: likewise the second direction, likewise the third direction, likewise the fourth direction,

rows.pali: Iti uddhamadho tiriyaṃ sabbadhi sabbattatāya sabbāvantaṃ lokaṃ mettāsahagatena cetasā vipulena mahaggatena appamāṇena averena abyāpajjena pharitvā viharati (vibha. 642; dī. ni. 1.556).
rows.english: and so above, below, and around; everywhere and equally he dwells pervading the entire world with his heart endued with loving- kindness, abundant, exalted, measureless, free from enmity, and free from affliction” (Vibh 272; D I 250).

rows.pali: Paṭhamajjhānādivasena appanāppattacittasseva hi ayaṃ vikubbanā sampajjati.
rows.english: For this versatility comes about only in one whose consciousness has reached absorption in the first jhāna and the rest.

rows.pali: 254.Ettha ca mettāsahagatenāti mettāya samannāgatena.
rows.english: 45. And here endued with loving-kindness means possessing loving-kindness.

rows.pali: Cetasāti cittena.
rows.english: With his heart (cetasā): with his mind (cittena).

rows.pali: Ekaṃ disanti ekamekissā disāya paṭhamapariggahitaṃ sattaṃ upādāya ekadisāpariyāpannasattapharaṇavasena vuttaṃ.
rows.english: One direction: this refers to anyone direction in which a being is first discerned and means pervasion of the beings included in that one direction.

rows.pali: Pharitvāti phusitvā ārammaṇaṃ katvā.
rows.english: Pervading: touching, making his object.

rows.pali: Viharatīti brahmavihārādhiṭṭhitaṃ iriyāpathavihāraṃ pavatteti.
rows.english: He dwells (viharati): he causes the occurrence of an abiding (vihāra—dwelling or continuation) in postures that is devoted to the divine abidings (see IV.103).

rows.pali: Tathā dutiyanti yathā puratthimādīsu disāsu yaṃkiñci ekaṃ disaṃ pharitvā viharati, tatheva tadanantaraṃ dutiyaṃ tatiyaṃ catutthañcāti attho.
rows.english: Likewise the second: just as he dwells pervading anyone direction among those beginning with the eastern one, so he does with the next one, and the third and the fourth, is the meaning.

rows.pali: Iti uddhanti eteneva nayena uparimaṃ disanti vuttaṃ hoti.
rows.english: 46. So above: in that same way in the upper direction is what is meant.

rows.pali: Adho tiriyanti adhodisampi tiriyaṃdisampi evameva.
rows.english: Below, around: so too the lower direction and the direction all round.

rows.pali: Tattha ca adhoti heṭṭhā.
rows.english: Herein, below is underneath,

rows.pali: Tiriyanti anudisāsu.
rows.english: and around is in the intermediate directions.

rows.pali: Evaṃ sabbadisāsu assamaṇḍale assamiva mettāsahagataṃ cittaṃ sāretipi paccāsāretipīti.
rows.english: So he sends his heart full of loving-kindness back and forth in all directions like a horse in a circus ground.

rows.pali: Ettāvatā ekaṃ disaṃ pariggahetvā odhiso mettāpharaṇaṃ dassitaṃ.
rows.english: Up to this point specified pervasion with loving-kindness is shown in the discernment of each direction separately.

rows.pali: Sabbadhītiādi pana anodhiso dassanatthaṃ vuttaṃ.
rows.english: 47.Everywhere, etc., is said for the purpose of showing unspecified pervasion.

rows.pali: Tattha sabbadhīti sabbattha.
rows.english: Herein, everywhere means in all places.

rows.pali: Sabbattatāyāti sabbesu hīnamajjhimukkaṭṭhamittasapattamajjhattādippabhedesu attatāya.
rows.english: Equally (sabbattatāya): to all classed as inferior, medium, superior, friendly, hostile, neutral, etc., just as to oneself (attatā);

rows.pali: "Ayaṃ parasatto"ti vibhāgaṃ akatvā attasamatāyāti vuttaṃ hoti.
rows.english: equality with oneself (atta-samatā) without making the distinction, “This is another being,” is what is meant.

rows.pali: Atha vā sabbattatāyāti sabbena cittabhāgena īsakampi bahi avikkhipamānoti vuttaṃ hoti.
rows.english: Or alternatively, equally (sabbattatāya) is with the whole state of the mind; not reserving even a little, is what is meant.

rows.pali: Sabbāvantanti sabbasattavantaṃ, sabbasattayuttanti attho.
rows.english: Entire (sabbāvant): possessing all beings (sabbasattavant); associated with all beings, is the meaning.

rows.pali: Lokanti sattalokaṃ.
rows.english: World is the world of beings.

rows.pali: Vipulenātievamādipariyāyadassanato panettha puna mettāsahagatenāti vuttaṃ.
rows.english: 48. Endued with loving-kindness is said again here in order to introduce the synonyms beginning with abundant.

rows.pali: Yasmā vā ettha odhiso pharaṇe viya puna tathāsaddo itisaddo vā na vutto, tasmā puna mettāsahagatena cetasāti vuttaṃ.
rows.english: Or alternatively, endued with loving-kindness is repeated because the word likewise or the word so is not repeated here as it was in the case of the [preceding] specified pervasion.

rows.pali: Nigamavasena vā etaṃ vuttaṃ.
rows.english: Or alternatively, it is said as a way of concluding.

rows.pali: Vipulenāti ettha ca pharaṇavasena vipulatā daṭṭhabbā.
rows.english: And abundant should be regarded here as abundance in pervading.

rows.pali: Bhūmivasena pana etaṃ mahaggataṃ paguṇavasena ca appamāṇasattārammaṇavasena ca appamāṇaṃ, byāpādapaccatthikappahānena averaṃ, domanassappahānato abyāpajjaṃ, niddukkhanti vuttaṃ hoti.
rows.english: But it is exalted in plane [from the sensual-sphere plane to the fine- material-sphere plane], measureless through familiarity and through having measureless beings as its object, free from enmity through abandonment of ill will and hostility, and free from affliction through abandonment of grief; without suffering, is what is meant.

rows.pali: Ayaṃ mettāsahagatena cetasātiādinā nayena vuttāya vikubbanāya attho.
rows.english: This is the meaning of the versatility described in the way beginning, “With his heart endued with loving-kindness.”

rows.pali: 255.Yathā cāyaṃ appanāppattacittasseva vikubbanā sampajjati, tathā yampi paṭisambhidāyaṃ (paṭi. ma. 2.22) "pañcahākārehi anodhisopharaṇā mettācetovimutti, sattahākārehi odhisopharaṇā mettā cetovimutti, dasahākārehi disāpharaṇā mettā cetovimuttī"ti vuttaṃ, tampi appanāppattacittasseva sampajjatīti veditabbaṃ.
rows.english: 49.And just as this versatility is successful only in one whose mind has reached absorption, so too that described in the Paṭisambhidā should be understood to be successful only in one whose mind has reached absorption, that is to say: “The mind-deliverance of loving-kindness is [practiced] with unspecified pervasion in five ways. The mind-deliverance of loving-kindness is [practiced] with specified pervasion in seven ways. The mind-deliverance of loving-kindness is [practiced] with directional pervasion in ten ways” (Paṭis II 130).

rows.pali: Tattha ca sabbe sattā averā abyāpajjā anīghā sukhī attānaṃ pariharantu, sabbe pāṇā, sabbe bhūtā, sabbe puggalā, sabbe attabhāvapariyāpannā averā - pe - pariharantūti imehi pañcahākārehi anodhisopharaṇā mettā cetovimutti veditabbā.
rows.english: 50. And herein, the mind-deliverance of loving-kindness is [practiced] with unspecified pervasion in these five ways: “May all beings be free from enmity, affliction and anxiety, and live happily. May all breathing things … all creatures … all persons … all those who have a personality be free from enmity, affliction and anxiety, and live happily” (Paṭis II 130).

rows.pali: Sabbā itthiyo averā - pe - attānaṃ pariharantu, sabbe purisā, sabbe ariyā, sabbe anariyā, sabbe devā, sabbe manussā, sabbe vinipātikā averā - pe - pariharantūti imehi sattahākārehi odhisopharaṇā mettā cetovimutti veditabbā.
rows.english: 51. The mind-deliverance of loving-kindness is [practiced] with specified pervasion in these seven ways: “May all women be free from enmity, affliction and anxiety and live happily. May all men … all Noble Ones … all not Noble Ones … all deities … all human beings … all in states of loss be free from enmity, affliction and anxiety, and live happily” (Paṭis II 131).

rows.pali: Sabbe puratthimāya disāya sattā averā - pe - attānaṃ pariharantu.
rows.english: 52. “May all beings in the eastern direction be free from enmity, affliction and anxiety, and live happily.

rows.pali: Sabbe pacchimāya disāya, sabbe uttarāya disāya, sabbe dakkhiṇāya disāya, sabbe puratthimāya anudisāya, sabbe pacchimāya anudisāya, sabbe uttarāya anudisāya, sabbe dakkhiṇāya anudisāya, sabbe heṭṭhimāya disāya, sabbe uparimāya disāya sattā averā - pe - pariharantu.
rows.english: May all beings in the western direction … northern direction … southern direction [310] … eastern intermediate direction … western intermediate direction … northern intermediate direction … southern intermediate direction … downward direction … upward direction be free from enmity, affliction and anxiety, and live happily.

rows.pali: Sabbe puratthimāya disāya pāṇā, bhūtā, puggalā, attabhāvapariyāpannā, averā - pe - pariharantu.
rows.english: May all breathing things in the eastern direction … May all creatures in the eastern direction … May all persons in the eastern direction … May all who have a personality in the eastern direction … [etc.] … in the upward direction be free from enmity, affliction and anxiety, and live happily.

rows.pali: Sabbā puratthimāya disāya itthiyo, sabbe purisā, ariyā, anariyā, devā, manussā, vinipātikā averā - pe - pariharantu.
rows.english: May all women in the eastern direction … May all men in the eastern direction … May all Noble Ones in the eastern direction … May all not Noble Ones in the eastern direction … May all deities in the eastern direction … May all human beings in the eastern direction … May all those in states of loss in the eastern direction … [etc.] … be free from enmity, affliction and anxiety, and live happily” (Paṭis II 131).

rows.pali: Sabbā pacchimāya disāya, uttarāya, dakkhiṇāya, puratthimāya anudisāya, pacchimāya, uttarāya, dakkhiṇāya anudisāya, heṭṭhimāya disāya, uparimāya disāya itthiyo - pe - vinipātikā averā abyāpajjā anīghā sukhī attānaṃ pariharantūti imehi dasahākārehi disāpharaṇā mettā cetovimutti veditabbā.
rows.english: The mind-deliverance of loving-kindness is [practiced] with directional pervasion in these ten ways (above).

rows.pali: 256.Tattha sabbeti anavasesapariyādānametaṃ.
rows.english: 53. Herein, all signifies inclusion without exception.

rows.pali: Sattāti rūpādīsu khandhesu chandarāgena sattā visattāti sattā.
rows.english: Beings (satta): they are held (satta), gripped (visatta) by desire and greed for the aggregates beginning with materiality, thus they are beings (satta).

rows.pali: Vuttañhetaṃ bhagavatā –
rows.english: For this is said by the Blessed One:

rows.pali: Ruḷhīsaddena pana vītarāgesupi ayaṃ vohāro vattatiyeva, vilīvamayepi bījanivisese tālavaṇṭavohāro viya.
rows.english: But in ordinary speech this term of common usage is applied also to those who are without greed, just as the term of common usage “palm fan” (tālavaṇṭa) is used for different sorts of fans [in general] even if made of split bamboo.

rows.pali: Akkharacintakā pana atthaṃ avicāretvā nāmamattametanti icchanti.
rows.english: However, [in the world] etymologists (akkhara-cintaka) who do not consider meaning have it that it is a mere name,

rows.pali: Yepi atthaṃ vicārenti, te satvayogena sattāti icchanti.
rows.english: while those who do consider meaning have it that a “being” (satta) is so called with reference to the “bright principle” (satta).6

rows.pali: Pāṇanatāya pāṇā, assāsapassāsāyattavuttitāyāti attho.
rows.english: 54.Breathing things (pāṇa): so called because of their state of breathing (pāṇanatā); the meaning is, because their existence depends on in-breaths and out-breaths.

rows.pali: Bhūtattā bhūtā, saṃbhūtattā abhinibbattattāti attho.
rows.english: Creatures (bhūta): so called because of being (bhūtatta = becomeness); the meaning is, because of their being fully become (sambhūtatta), because of their being generated (abhinibbattatta).

rows.pali: Punti vuccati nirayo.
rows.english: “puṃ” is what hell is called;

rows.pali: Tasmiṃ galantīti puggalā, gacchantīti attho.
rows.english: they fall (galanti) into that, is the meaning of persons (puggala).

rows.pali: Attabhāvo vuccati sarīraṃ.
rows.english: Personality (attabhāva) is what the physical body is called;

rows.pali: Khandhapañcakameva vā, tamupādāya paññattimattasambhavato.
rows.english: or it is just the pentad of aggregates, since it is actually only a concept derived from that pentad of aggregates7

rows.pali: Tasmiṃ attabhāve pariyāpannāti attabhāvapariyāpannā.
rows.english: [What is referred to is] included (pariyāpanna) in that personality, thus it “has a personality” (attabhāva- pariyāpanna).

rows.pali: Pariyāpannāti paricchinnā, antogadhāti attho.
rows.english: “Included in” is delimited by; “gone into” is the meaning.

rows.pali: Yathā ca sattāti vacanaṃ, evaṃ sesānipi ruḷhīvasena āropetvā sabbānetāni sabbasattavevacanānīti veditabbāni.
rows.english: 55.And all the remaining [terms] should be understood as synonyms for “all beings” used in accordance with ordinary speech as in the case of the term “beings.”

rows.pali: Kāmañca aññānipi sabbe jantū sabbe jīvātiādīni sabbasattavevacanāni atthi, pākaṭavasena pana imāneva pañca gahetvā "pañcahākārehi anodhisopharaṇā mettā cetovimuttī"ti vuttaṃ.
rows.english: Of course, [311] there are other synonyms too for all “beings,” such as all “folks,” all “souls,” etc.; still it is for clarity’s sake that “The mind-deliverance of loving-kindness is [practiced] with unspecified pervasion in five ways” is said and that only these five are mentioned.

rows.pali: Ye pana sattā pāṇātiādīnaṃ na kevalaṃ vacanamattatova, atha kho atthatopi nānattameva iccheyyuṃ, tesaṃ anodhisopharaṇā virujjhati, tasmā tathā atthaṃ agahetvā imesu pañcasu ākāresu aññataravasena anodhiso mettā pharitabbā.
rows.english: 56. Those who would have it that there is not only a mere verbal difference between “beings,” “breathing things,” etc., but also an actual difference in meaning, are contradicted by the mention of unspecified pervasion. So instead of taking the meaning in that way, the unspecified pervasion with loving- kindness is done in any one of these five ways.

rows.pali: 257.Ettha ca sabbe sattā averā hontūti ayamekā appanā.
rows.english: And here, may all beings be free from enmity is one absorption;

rows.pali: Abyāpajjā hontūti ayamekā appanā.
rows.english: free from affliction is one absorption—

rows.pali: Abyāpajjāti byāpādarahitā.
rows.english: free from affliction (abyābajjha) is free from afflictedness (byābādha-rahita);8

rows.pali: Anīghā hontūti ayamekā appanā.
rows.english: free from anxiety is one absorption—

rows.pali: Anīghāti niddukkhā.
rows.english: free from anxiety is free from suffering;

rows.pali: Sukhī attānaṃ pariharantūti ayamekā appanā.
rows.english: may they live happily is one absorption.

rows.pali: Tasmā imesupi padesu yaṃ yaṃ pākaṭaṃ hoti, tassa tassa vasena mettā pharitabbā.
rows.english: Consequently he should do his pervading with loving-kindness according to whichever of these phrases is clear to him.

rows.pali: Iti pañcasu ākāresu catunnaṃ appanānaṃ vasena anodhisopharaṇe vīsati appanā honti.
rows.english: So with the four kinds of absorption in each of the five ways, there are twenty kinds of absorption in unspecified pervasion.

rows.pali: Odhisopharaṇe pana sattasu ākāresu catunnaṃ vasena aṭṭhavīsati.
rows.english: 57. In specified pervasion, with the four kinds of absorption in each of the seven ways, there are twenty-eight kinds of absorption.

rows.pali: Ettha ca itthiyo purisāti liṅgavasena vuttaṃ.
rows.english: And here “woman” and “man” are stated according to sex;

rows.pali: Ariyā anariyāti ariyaputhujjanavasena.
rows.english: “Noble Ones” and “not Noble Ones” according to Noble Ones and ordinary people;

rows.pali: Devā manussā vinipātikāti upapattivasena.
rows.english: “deities” and “human beings” and “those in states of loss” according to the kind of rebirth.

rows.pali: Disāpharaṇe pana sabbe puratthimāya disāya sattātiādinā nayena ekamekissā disāya vīsati vīsati katvā dvesatāni, sabbā puratthimāya disāya itthiyotiādinā nayena ekamekissā disāya aṭṭhavīsati aṭṭhavīsati katvā asīti dvesatānīti cattāri satāni asīti ca appanā.
rows.english: 58. In directional pervasion, with twenty kinds of absorption in each of the directions beginning with “all beings in the eastern direction,” there are two hundred kinds of absorption; and with twenty-eight kinds in each of the directions beginning with “all women in the eastern direction” there are two hundred and eighty kinds; so these make four hundred and eighty kinds of absorption.

rows.pali: Iti sabbānipi paṭisambhidāyaṃ vuttāni aṭṭhavīsādhikāni pañca appanāsatānīti.
rows.english: Consequently all the kinds of absorption mentioned in the Paṭisambhidā amount to five hundred and twenty-eight.

rows.pali: Iti etāsu appanāsu yassa kassaci vasena mettaṃ cetovimuttiṃ bhāvetvā ayaṃ yogāvacaro "sukhaṃ supatī"tiādinā nayena vutte ekādasānisaṃse paṭilabhati.
rows.english: 59.So when this meditator develops the mind-deliverance of loving-kindness through any one of these kinds of absorption, he obtains the eleven advantages described in the way beginning, “A man sleeps in comfort” (§37).

rows.pali: 258.Tattha sukhaṃ supatīti yathā sesā janā samparivattamānā kākacchamānā dukkhaṃ supanti, evaṃ asupitvā sukhaṃ supati.
rows.english: 60. Herein, sleeps in comfort means that instead of sleeping uncomfortably, turning over and snoring as other people do, he sleeps comfortably,

rows.pali: Niddaṃ okkantopi samāpattiṃ samāpanno viya hoti.
rows.english: he falls asleep as though entering upon an attainment.

rows.pali: Sukhaṃ paṭibujjhatīti yathā aññe nitthunantā vijambhantā samparivattantā dukkhaṃ paṭibujjhanti, evaṃ appaṭibujjhitvā vikasamānamiva padumaṃ sukhaṃ nibbikāraṃ paṭibujjhati.
rows.english: 61. He wakes in comfort: instead of waking uncomfortably, groaning and yawning and turning over as others do, he wakes comfortably without contortions, like a lotus opening. [312]

rows.pali: Napāpakaṃ supinaṃ passatīti supinaṃ passantopi bhaddakameva supinaṃ passati, cetiyaṃ vandanto viya pūjaṃ karonto viya dhammaṃ suṇanto viya ca hoti.
rows.english: 62. He dreams no evil dreams: when he sees dreams, he sees only auspicious ones, as though he were worshipping a shrine, as though he were making an offering, as though he were hearing the Dhamma.

rows.pali: Yathā pana aññe attānaṃ corehi samparivāritaṃ viya vāḷehi upaddutaṃ viya papāte patantaṃ viya ca passanti, evaṃ pāpakaṃ supinaṃ na passati.
rows.english: But he does not see evil dreams as others do, as though being surrounded by bandits, as though being threatened by wild beasts, as though falling into chasms (see XIV, n. 45).

rows.pali: Manussānaṃ piyo hotīti ure āmuttamuttāhāro viya sīse piḷandhamālā viya ca manussānaṃ piyo hoti manāpo.
rows.english: 63.He is dear to human beings: he is as dear to and beloved by human beings as a necklace worn to hang on the chest, as a wreath adorning the head.

rows.pali: Amanussānaṃ piyo hotīti yatheva manussānaṃ, evaṃ amanussānampi piyo hoti visākhatthero viya.
rows.english: 64.He is dear to non-human beings: he is just as dear to non-human beings as he is to human beings, as in the Elder Visākha’s case.

rows.pali: So kira pāṭaliputte kuṭumbiyo ahosi.
rows.english: He was a landowner, it seems, at Pāṭaliputta (Patna).

rows.pali: So tattheva vasamāno assosi "tambapaṇṇidīpo kira cetiyamālālaṅkato kāsāvapajjoto icchiticchitaṭṭhāneyeva ettha sakkā nisīdituṃ vā nipajjituṃ vā utusappāyaṃ senāsanasappāyaṃ puggalasappāyaṃ dhammassavanasappāyanti sabbamettha sulabha"nti.
rows.english: While he was living there he heard this: “The Island of Tambapaṇṇi (Sri Lanka), apparently, is adorned with a diadem of shrines and gleams with the yellow cloth, and there a man can sit or lie wherever he likes; there the climate is favourable, the abodes are favourable, the people are favourable, the Dhamma to be heard is favourable, and all these favourable things are easily obtained there.”

rows.pali: So attano bhogakkhandhaṃ puttadārassa niyyādetvā dussante baddhena ekakahāpaṇeneva gharā nikkhamitvā samuddatīre nāvaṃ udikkhamāno ekamāsaṃ vasi.
rows.english: 65.He made over his fortune to his wife and children and left his home with only a single ducat (kahāpaṇa) sewn into the hem of his garment. He stopped for one month on the sea coast in expectation of a ship,

rows.pali: So vohārakusalatāya imasmiṃ ṭhāne bhaṇḍaṃ kiṇitvā asukasmiṃ vikkiṇanto dhammikāya vaṇijjāya tenevantaramāsena sahassaṃ abhisaṃhari.
rows.english: and meanwhile by his skill in trading he made a thousand during the month by buying goods here and selling them there in lawful enterprise.

rows.pali: Anupubbena mahāvihāraṃ āgantvā pabbajjaṃ yāci.
rows.english: 66. Eventually he came to the Great Monastery [(Mahāvihāra) at Anurādha- pura], and there he asked for the going forth into homelessness.

rows.pali: So pabbājanatthāya sīmaṃ nīto taṃ sahassatthavikaṃ ovaṭṭikantarena bhūmiyaṃ pātesi.
rows.english: When he was being conducted to the chapter house (sīmā) for the going-forth ceremony, the purse containing the thousand pieces dropped out from under his belt.

rows.pali: "Kimeta"nti ca vutte "kahāpaṇasahassaṃ, bhante"ti vatvā "upāsaka, pabbajitakālato paṭṭhāya na sakkā vicāretuṃ, idānevetaṃ vicārehī"ti vutte "visākhassa pabbajjaṭṭhānamāgatā mā rittahatthā gamiṃsū"ti muñcitvā sīmāmāḷake vippakiritvā pabbajitvā upasampanno.
rows.english: When asked “What is that?” he replied, “It is a thousand ducats, venerable sirs.” They told him, “Lay follower, it is not possible to distribute them after the going forth. Distribute them now.” Then he said, “Let none who have come to the scene of Visākha’s going forth depart empty-handed,” and opening [the purse] he strewed them over the chapter house yard, after which he received the going forth and the full admission.

rows.pali: So pañcavasso hutvā dvemātikā paguṇā katvā pavāretvā attano sappāyaṃ kammaṭṭhānaṃ gahetvā ekekasmiṃ vihāre cattāro māse katvā samappavattavāsaṃ vasamāno cari.
rows.english: 67.When he had acquired five years’ seniority and had become familiar with the two Codes (Pātimokkha; see III.31) he celebrated the Pavāraṇā at the end of the Rains, took a meditation subject that suited him, and set out to wander, living for four months in each monastery and doing the duties on a basis of equality with the residents.

rows.pali: Evaṃ caramāno –
rows.english: While he was wandering in this way:

rows.pali: So cittalapabbatavihāraṃ gacchanto dvedhā pathaṃ patvā "ayaṃ nu kho maggo udāhu aya"nti cintayanto aṭṭhāsi.
rows.english: 68.On his way to Cittalapabbata he came to a road fork and stood wondering which turn to take.

rows.pali: Athassa pabbate adhivatthā devatā hatthaṃ pasāretvā "esa maggo"ti vatvā dasseti.
rows.english: Then a deity living in a rock held out a hand pointing out the road to him.

rows.pali: So cittalapabbatavihāraṃ gantvā tattha cattāro māse vasitvā paccūse gamissāmīti cintetvā nipajji.
rows.english: 69. He came to the Cittalapabbata Monastery. After he had stayed there for four months he lay down thinking, “In the morning I depart.”

rows.pali: Caṅkamasīse maṇilarukkhe adhivatthā devatā sopānaphalake nisīditvā parodi.
rows.english: Then a deity living in a maṇila tree at the end of the walk sat down on a step of the stair and burst into tears.

rows.pali: Thero "ko eso"ti āha.
rows.english: The elder asked, “Who is that?”

rows.pali: Ahaṃ, bhante, maṇiliyāti.
rows.english: —“It is I, Maṇiliyā, venerable sir.”

rows.pali: Kissa rodasīti?
rows.english: —“What are you weeping for?”

rows.pali: Tumhākaṃ gamanaṃ paṭiccāti.
rows.english: —“Because you are going away.”

rows.pali: Mayi idha vasante tumhākaṃ ko guṇoti?
rows.english: —“What good does my living here to you?”

rows.pali: Tumhesu, bhante, idha vasantesu amanussā aññamaññaṃ mettaṃ paṭilabhanti, te dāni tumhesu gatesu kalahaṃ karissanti, duṭṭhullampi kathayissantīti.
rows.english: —“Venerable sir, as long as you live here non- human beings treat each other kindly. Now, when you are gone, they will start quarrels and loose talk.”9

rows.pali: Thero "sace mayi idha vasante tumhākaṃ phāsuvihāro hoti, sundara"nti vatvā aññepi cattāro māse tattheva vasitvā puna tatheva gamanacittaṃ uppādesi.
rows.english: The elder said, “If my living here makes you live at peace, that is good,” and so he stayed there another four months. Then he again thought of leaving,

rows.pali: Devatāpi puna tatheva parodi.
rows.english: but the deity wept as before.

rows.pali: Etenevupāyena thero tattheva vasitvā tattheva parinibbāyīti evaṃ mettāvihārī bhikkhu amanussānaṃ piyo hoti.
rows.english: And so the elder lived on there, and it was there that he attained Nibbāna. This is how a bhikkhu who abides in loving-kindness is dear to non-human beings.

rows.pali: Devatā rakkhantīti puttamiva mātāpitaro devatā rakkhanti.
rows.english: 70. Deities guard him: deities guard him as a mother and father guard their child.

rows.pali: Nāssaaggi vā visaṃ vā satthaṃ vā kamatīti mettāvihārissa kāye uttarāya upāsikāya viya aggi vā, saṃyuttabhāṇakacūḷasivattherasseva visaṃ vā, saṃkiccasāmaṇerasseva satthaṃ vā na kamati, na pavisati.
rows.english: 71.Fire, poison and weapons do not affect him: they do not affect, do not enter into, the body of one who abides in loving-kindness, like the fire in the case of the lay woman devotee Uttarā (see XII.34 and Dhp-a III 310), like the poison in the case of the Saṃyutta reciter the Elder Cūḷa-Siva, like the knife in the case of the novice Saṅkicca (see Dhp-a II 249);

rows.pali: Nāssa kāyaṃ vikopetīti vuttaṃ hoti.
rows.english: they do not disturb the body, is what is meant.

rows.pali: Dhenuvatthumpi cettha kathayanti.
rows.english: 72.And they tell the story of the cow here too.

rows.pali: Ekā kira dhenu vacchakassa khīradhāraṃ muñcamānā aṭṭhāsi.
rows.english: A cow was giving milk to her calf, it seems.

rows.pali: Eko luddako taṃ vijjhissāmīti hatthena samparivattetvā dīghadaṇḍasattiṃ muñci.
rows.english: A hunter, thinking “I shall shoot her,” flourished a long-handled spear in his hand and flung it.

rows.pali: Sā tassā sarīraṃ āhacca tālapaṇṇaṃ viya pavaṭṭamānā gatā, neva upacārabalena, na appanābalena, kevalaṃ vacchake balavapiyacittatāya.
rows.english: It struck her body and bounced off like a palm leaf—and that was owing neither to access nor to absorption, but simply to the strength of her consciousness of love for her calf.

rows.pali: Evaṃ mahānubhāvā mettāti.
rows.english: So mightily powerful is loving- kindness.

rows.pali: Tuvaṭaṃ cittaṃ samādhiyatīti mettāvihārino khippameva cittaṃ samādhiyati, natthi tassa dandhāyitattaṃ.
rows.english: 73.His mind is easily concentrated: the mind of one who abides in loving-kindness is quickly concentrated, there is no sluggishness about it. [314]

rows.pali: Mukhavaṇṇo vippasīdatīti bandhanā pavuttaṃ tālapakkaṃ viya cassa vippasannavaṇṇaṃ mukhaṃ hoti.
rows.english: 74. The expression of his face is serene: his face has a serene expression, like a palmyra fruit loosed from its stem.

rows.pali: Asammūḷho kālaṅkarotīti mettāvihārino sammohamaraṇaṃ nāma natthi, asammūḷhova niddaṃ okkamanto viya kālaṃ karoti.
rows.english: 75.He dies unconfused: there is no dying deluded for one who abides in loving- kindness. He passes away undeluded as if falling asleep.

rows.pali: Uttarimappaṭivijjhantoti mettāsamāpattito uttariṃ arahattaṃ adhigantuṃ asakkonto ito cavitvā suttappabuddho viya brahmalokamupapajjatīti.
rows.english: 76.If he penetrates no higher: if he is unable to reach higher than the attainment of loving-kindness and attain Arahantship, then when he falls from this life, he reappears in the Brahmā-world as one who wakes up from sleep.

rows.pali: Ayaṃ mettābhāvanāyaṃ vitthārakathā.
rows.english: This is the detailed explanation of the development of loving-kindness.

rows.pali: 259.Karuṇaṃ bhāvetukāmena pana nikkaruṇatāya ādīnavaṃ karuṇāya ca ānisaṃsaṃ paccavekkhitvā karuṇābhāvanā ārabhitabbā.
rows.english: 77.One who wants to develop compassion should begin his task by reviewing the danger in lack of compassion and the advantage in compassion.

rows.pali: Tañca pana ārabhantena paṭhamaṃ piyapuggalādīsu na ārabhitabbā.
rows.english: And when he begins it, he should not direct it at first towards the dear, etc., persons;

rows.pali: Piyo hi piyaṭṭhāneyeva tiṭṭhati.
rows.english: for one who is dear simply retains the position of one who is dear,

rows.pali: Atippiyasahāyako atippiyasahāyakaṭṭhāneyeva.
rows.english: a very dear companion retains the position of a very dear companion,

rows.pali: Majjhatto majjhattaṭṭhāneyeva.
rows.english: one who is neutral retains the position of one who is neutral,

rows.pali: Appiyo appiyaṭṭhāneyeva.
rows.english: one who is antipathetic retains the position of one who is antipathetic,

rows.pali: Verī veriṭṭhāneyeva tiṭṭhati.
rows.english: and one who is hostile retains the position of one who is hostile.

rows.pali: Liṅgavisabhāgakālakatā akhettameva.
rows.english: One of the opposite sex and one who is dead are also not the field for it.

rows.pali: "Kathañca bhikkhu karuṇāsahagatena cetasā ekaṃ disaṃ pharitvā viharati?
rows.english: 78.In the Vibhaṅga it is said: “And how does a bhikkhu dwell pervading one direction with his heart endued with compassion?

rows.pali: Seyyathāpi nāma ekaṃ puggalaṃ duggataṃ durūpetaṃ disvā karuṇāyeyya, evameva sabbasatte karuṇāya pharatī"ti vibhaṅge (vibha. 653) pana vuttattā sabbapaṭhamaṃ tāva kiñcideva karuṇāyitabbarūpaṃ paramakicchappattaṃ duggataṃ durūpetaṃ kapaṇapurisaṃ chinnāhāraṃ kapālaṃ purato ṭhapetvā anāthasālāya nisinnaṃ hatthapādehi paggharantakimigaṇaṃ aṭṭassaraṃ karontaṃ disvā "kicchaṃ vatāyaṃ satto āpanno, appeva nāma imamhā dukkhā mucceyyā"ti karuṇā pavattetabbā.
rows.english: Just as he would feel compassion on seeing an unlucky, unfortunate person, so he pervades all beings with compassion” (Vibh 273). Therefore first of all, on seeing a wretched man, unlucky, unfortunate, in every way a fit object for compassion, unsightly, reduced to utter misery, with hands and feet cut off, sitting in the shelter for the helpless with a pot placed before him, with a mass of maggots oozing from his arms and legs, and moaning, compassion should be felt for him in this way: “This being has indeed been reduced to misery; if only he could be freed from this suffering! ”

rows.pali: Taṃ alabhantena sukhitopi pāpakārī puggalo vajjhena upametvā karuṇāyitabbo.
rows.english: But if he does not encounter such a person, then he can arouse compassion for an evil-doing person, even though he is happy, by comparing him to one about to be executed.

rows.pali: Kathaṃ?
rows.english: How?

rows.pali: Seyyathāpi saha bhaṇḍena gahitacoraṃ "vadhetha na"nti rañño āṇāya rājapurisā bandhitvā catukke catukke pahārasatāni dentā āghātanaṃ nenti.
rows.english: 79. Suppose a robber has been caught with stolen goods, and in accordance with the king’s command to execute him, the king’s men bind him and lead him off to the place of execution, giving him a hundred blows in sets of four.

rows.pali: Tassa manussā khādanīyampi bhojanīyampi mālāgandhavilepanatambulānipi denti.
rows.english: Then people give him things to chew and eat and also garlands and perfumes, unguents and betel leaves.

rows.pali: Kiñcāpi so tāni khādanto ceva paribhuñjanto ca sukhito bhogasamappito viya gacchati, atha kho taṃ neva koci "sukhito ayaṃ mahābhogo"ti maññati, aññadatthu "ayaṃ varāko idāni marissati, yaṃ yadeva hi ayaṃ padaṃ nikkhipati, tena tena santike maraṇassa hotī"ti taṃ jano karuṇāyati.
rows.english: Although he goes along eating and enjoying these things as though he were happy and well off, still no one fancies that he is really happy and well off. On the contrary people feel compassion for him, thinking, “This poor wretch is now about to die; every step he takes brings him nearer to the presence of death.”

rows.pali: Evameva karuṇākammaṭṭhānikena bhikkhunā sukhitopi puggalo evaṃ karuṇāyitabbo "ayaṃ varāko kiñcāpi idāni sukhito susajjito bhoge paribhuñjati, atha kho tīsu dvāresu ekenāpi katassa kalyāṇakammassa abhāvā idāni apāyesu anappakaṃ dukkhaṃ domanassaṃ paṭisaṃvedissatī"ti.
rows.english: So too a bhikkhu whose meditation subject is compassion should arouse compassion for an [evil-doing] person even if he is happy: “Though this poor wretch is now happy, cheerful, enjoying his wealth, still for want of even one good deed done now in any one of the three doors [of body, speech and mind] he can come to experience untold suffering in the states of loss.”

rows.pali: Evaṃ taṃ puggalaṃ karuṇāyitvā tato paraṃ eteneva upāyena piyapuggale, tato majjhatte, tato verimhīti anukkamena karuṇā pavattetabbā.
rows.english: 80. Having aroused compassion for that person in that way, he should next arouse compassion for a dear person, next for a neutral person, and next for a hostile person, successively in the same way.

rows.pali: Sace panassa pubbe vuttanayeneva verimhi paṭighaṃ uppajjati, taṃ mettāya vuttanayeneva vūpasametabbaṃ.
rows.english: 81. But if resentment towards the hostile person arises in the way already described, he should make it subside in the way described under loving-kindness (§§14–39).

rows.pali: Yopi cettha katakusalo hoti, tampi ñātirogabhogabyasanādīnaṃ aññatarena byasanena samannāgataṃ disvā vā sutvā vā tesaṃ abhāvepi vaṭṭadukkhaṃ anatikkantattā "dukkhitova aya"nti evaṃ sabbathāpi karuṇāyitvā vuttanayeneva attani piyapuggale majjhatte verimhīti catūsu janesu sīmāsambhedaṃ katvā taṃ nimittaṃ āsevantena bhāventena bahulīkarontena mettāya vuttanayeneva tikacatukkajjhānavasena appanā vaḍḍhetabbā.
rows.english: And here too when someone has done profitable deeds and the meditator sees or hears that he has been overtaken by one of the kinds of ruin beginning with ruin of health, relatives, property, etc., he deserves the meditator’s compassion; and so he does too in any case, even with no such ruin, thus “In reality he is unhappy,” because he is not exempt from the suffering of the round [of becoming]. And in the way already described the meditator should break down the barriers between the four kinds of people, that is to say, himself, the dear person, the neutral person and the hostile person. Then cultivating that sign, developing it and repeatedly practicing it, he should increase the absorption by the triple and quadruple jhāna in the way already stated under loving-kindness.

rows.pali: Aṅguttaraṭṭhakathāyaṃ pana paṭhamaṃ veripuggalo karuṇāyitabbo, tasmiṃ cittaṃ muduṃ katvā duggato, tato piyapuggalo, tato attāti ayaṃ kamo vutto, so "duggataṃ durūpeta"nti pāḷiyā na sameti, tasmā vuttanayenevettha bhāvanamārabhitvā sīmāsambhedaṃ katvā appanā vaḍḍhetabbā.
rows.english: 82.But the order given in the Aṅguttara Commentary is that a hostile person should first be made the object of compassion, and when the mind has been made malleable with respect to him, next the unlucky person, next the dear person, and next oneself. That does not agree with the text, “an unlucky, unfortunate person” (§78).Therefore he should begin the development, break down the barriers, and increase absorption only in the way stated here.

rows.pali: Tato paraṃ "pañcahākārehi anodhisopharaṇā sattahākārehi odhisopharaṇā dasahākārehi disāpharaṇā"ti ayaṃ vikubbanā, "sukhaṃ supatī"tiādayo ānisaṃsā ca mettāyaṃ vuttanayeneva veditabbāti.
rows.english: 83. After that, the versatility consisting in the unspecified pervasion in five ways, the specified pervasion in seven ways, and the directional pervasion in ten ways, and the advantages described as “He sleeps in comfort,” etc., should be understood in the same way as given under loving-kindness.

rows.pali: Ayaṃ karuṇābhāvanāya vitthārakathā.
rows.english: This is the detailed explanation of the development of compassion.

rows.pali: 260.Muditābhāvanaṃ ārabhantenāpi na paṭhamaṃ piyapuggalādīsu ārabhitabbā.
rows.english: 84.One who begins the development of gladness10 should not start with the dear person and the rest;

rows.pali: Na hi piyo piyabhāvamatteneva muditāya padaṭṭhānaṃ hoti, pageva majjhattaverino.
rows.english: for a dear person is not the proximate cause of gladness merely in virtue of dearness, how much less the neutral and the hostile person.

rows.pali: Liṅgavisabhāgakālakatā akhettameva.
rows.english: One of the opposite sex and one who is dead are also not the field for it.

rows.pali: Atippiyasahāyako pana siyā padaṭṭhānaṃ, yo aṭṭhakathāyaṃ soṇḍasahāyoti vutto.
rows.english: 85.However, the very dear companion can be the proximate cause for it—one who in the commentaries is called a “boon companion,”

rows.pali: So hi muditamuditova hoti, paṭhamaṃ hasitvā pacchā katheti, tasmā so vā paṭhamaṃ muditāya pharitabbo.
rows.english: for he is constantly glad: he laughs first and speaks afterwards. So he should be the first to be pervaded with gladness.

rows.pali: Piyapuggalaṃ vā sukhitaṃ sajjitaṃ modamānaṃ disvā vā sutvā vā "modati vatāyaṃ satto, aho sādhu aho suṭṭhū"ti muditā uppādetabbā.
rows.english: Or on seeing or hearing about a dear person being happy, cheerful and glad, gladness can be aroused thus: “This being is indeed glad. How good, how excellent!”

rows.pali: Imameva hi atthavasaṃ paṭicca vibhaṅge (vibha. 663) vuttaṃ "kathañca bhikkhu muditāsahagatena cetasā ekaṃ disaṃ pharitvā viharati?
rows.english: For this is what is referred to in the Vibhaṅga: “And how does a bhikkhu dwell pervading one direction with his heart endued with gladness?

rows.pali: Seyyathāpi nāma ekaṃ puggalaṃ piyaṃ manāpaṃ disvā mudito assa, evameva sabbasatte muditāya pharatī"ti.
rows.english: Just as he would be glad on seeing a dear and beloved person, so he pervades all beings with gladness” (Vibh 274).

rows.pali: Sacepissa so soṇḍasahāyo vā piyapuggalo vā atīte sukhito ahosi, sampati pana duggato durūpeto, atītameva cassa sukhitabhāvaṃ anussaritvā "esa atīte evaṃ mahābhogo mahāparivāro niccappamudito ahosī"ti tamevassa muditākāraṃ gahetvā muditā uppādetabbā "anāgate vā pana puna taṃ sampattiṃ labhitvā hatthikkhandhaassapiṭṭhisuvaṇṇasivikādīhi vicarissatī"ti anāgatampissa muditākāraṃ gahetvā muditā uppādetabbā.
rows.english: 86.But if his boon companion or the dear person was happy in the past but is now unlucky and unfortunate, then gladness can still be aroused by remembering his past happiness and apprehending the glad aspect in this way: “In the past he had great wealth, a great following and he was always glad.” Or gladness can be aroused by apprehending the future glad aspect in him in this way: “In the future he will again enjoy similar success and will go about in gold palanquins, on the backs of elephants or on horseback, and so on.”

rows.pali: Evaṃ piyapuggale muditaṃ uppādetvā atha majjhatte tato verimhīti anukkamena muditā pavattetabbā.
rows.english: Having thus aroused gladness with respect to a dear person, he can then direct it successively towards a neutral one, and after that towards a hostile one.

rows.pali: Appanā vaḍḍhetabbā.

Sace panassa pubbe vuttanayeneva verimhi paṭighaṃ uppajjati, taṃ mettāyaṃ vuttanayeneva vūpasametvā "imesu ca tīsu attani cā"ti catūsu janesu samacittatāya sīmāsambhedaṃ katvā taṃ nimittaṃ āsevantena bhāventena bahulīkarontena mettāyaṃ vuttanayeneva tikacatukkajjhānavaseneva appanā vaḍḍhetabbā.
rows.english: 87.But if resentment towards the hostile one arises in him in the way already described, he should make it subside in the same way as described under loving- kindness (§§14–39). He should break down the barriers by means of mental impartiality towards the four, that is, towards these three and himself. And by cultivating that sign, developing and repeatedly practicing it, he should increase the absorption to triple and quadruple jhāna in the way already stated under loving-kindness.

rows.pali: Tato paraṃ "pañcahākārehi anodhisopharaṇā sattahākārehi odhisopharaṇā dasahākārehi disāpharaṇā"ti ayaṃ vikubbanā, "sukhaṃ supatī"tiādayo ānisaṃsā ca mettāyaṃ vuttanayeneva veditabbāti.
rows.english: Next, the versatility consisting in unspecified pervasion in five ways, specified pervasion in seven ways, and directional pervasion in ten ways, and also the advantages described as “He sleeps in comfort,” etc., should be understood in the same way as stated under loving-kindness.

rows.pali: Ayaṃ muditābhāvanāya vitthārakathā.
rows.english: This is the detailed explanation of the development of gladness.

rows.pali: 261.Upekkhābhāvanaṃ bhāvetukāmena pana mettādīsu paṭiladdhatikacatukkajjhānena paguṇatatiyajjhānā vuṭṭhāya "sukhitā hontū"tiādivasena sattakelāyanamanasikārayuttattā, paṭighānunayasamīpacārittā, somanassayogena oḷārikattā ca purimāsu ādīnavaṃ, santasabhāvattā upekkhāya ānisaṃsañca disvā yvāssa pakatimajjhatto puggalo, taṃ ajjhupekkhitvā upekkhā uppādetabbā.
rows.english: 88. One who wants to develop equanimity must have already obtained the triple or quadruple jhāna in loving-kindness, and so on. He should emerge from the third jhāna [in the fourfold reckoning], after he has made it familiar, and he should see danger in the former [three divine abidings] because they are linked with attention given to beings’ enjoyment in the way beginning “May they be happy,” because resentment and approval are near, and because their association with joy is gross. And he should also see the advantage in equanimity because it is peaceful. Then he should arouse equanimity (upekkhā) by looking on with equanimity (ajjhupekkhitvā) at a person who is normally neutral;

rows.pali: Tato piyapuggalādīsu.
rows.english: after that at a dear person, and the rest.

rows.pali: Vuttañhetaṃ "kathañca bhikkhu upekkhāsahagatena cetasā ekaṃ disaṃ pharitvā viharati?
rows.english: For this is said: “And how does a bhikkhu dwell pervading one direction with his heart endued with equanimity?

rows.pali: Seyyathāpi nāma ekaṃ puggalaṃ neva manāpaṃ na amanāpaṃ disvā upekkhako assa, evameva sabbe satte upekkhāya pharatī"ti (vibha. 673).
rows.english: Just as he would feel equanimity on seeing a person who was neither beloved nor unloved, so he pervades all beings with equanimity” (Vibh 275).

rows.pali: Tasmā vuttanayena majjhattapuggale upekkhaṃ uppādetvā atha piyapuggale, tato soṇḍasahāyake, tato verimhīti evaṃ "imesu ca tīsu attani cā"ti sabbattha majjhattavasena sīmāsambhedaṃ katvā taṃ nimittaṃ āsevitabbaṃ bhāvetabbaṃ bahulīkātabbaṃ.
rows.english: 89.Therefore he should arouse equanimity towards the neutral person in the way already stated. Then, through the neutral one, he should break down the barriers in each case between the three people, that is, the dear person, then the boon companion, and then the hostile one, and lastly himself. And he should cultivate that sign, develop and repeatedly practice it.

rows.pali: Tassevaṃ karoto pathavīkasiṇe vuttanayeneva catutthajjhānaṃ uppajjati.
rows.english: 90.As he does so the fourth jhāna arises in him in the way described under the earth kasiṇa.

rows.pali: Kiṃ panetaṃ pathavīkasiṇādīsu uppannatatiyajjhānassāpi uppajjatīti?
rows.english: But how then? Does this arise in one in whom the third jhāna has already arisen on the basis of the earth kasiṇa, etc.?

rows.pali: Nuppajjati.
rows.english: It does not.

rows.pali: Kasmā?
rows.english: Why not?

rows.pali: Ārammaṇavisabhāgatāya.
rows.english: Because of the dissimilarity of the object.

rows.pali: Mettādīsu uppannatatiyajjhānasseva pana uppajjati, ārammaṇasabhāgatāyāti.
rows.english: It arises only in one in whom the third jhāna has arisen on the basis of loving-kindness, etc., because the object is similar.

rows.pali: Tato paraṃ pana vikubbanā ca ānisaṃsapaṭilābho ca mettāyaṃ vuttanayeneva veditabboti.
rows.english: But after that the versatility and the obtaining of advantages should be understood in the same way as described under loving-kindness.

rows.pali: Ayaṃ upekkhābhāvanāya vitthārakathā.
rows.english: This is the detailed explanation of the development of equanimity.

rows.pali: Etāsu hi mettākaruṇāmuditāupekkhāsu atthato tāva mejjatīti mettā, siniyhatīti attho.
rows.english: [Meanings] 92. Now, as to the meaning firstly of loving-kindness, compassion, gladness and equanimity: it fattens (mejjati), thus it is loving-kindness (mettā); it is solvent (siniyhati) is the meaning.

rows.pali: Mitte vā bhavā, mittassa vā esā pavattītipi mettā.
rows.english: Also: it comes about with respect to a friend (mitta), [318] or it is behaviour towards a friend, thus it is loving-kindness (mettā).

rows.pali: Paradukkhe sati sādhūnaṃ hadayakampanaṃ karotīti karuṇā.
rows.english: When there is suffering in others it causes (karoti) good people’s hearts to be moved (kampana), thus it is compassion (karuṇā).

rows.pali: Kiṇāti vā paradukkhaṃ hiṃsati vināsetīti karuṇā.
rows.english: Or alternatively, it combats (kiṇāti)11 others’ suffering, attacks and demolishes it, thus it is compassion.

rows.pali: Kiriyati vā dukkhitesu pharaṇavasena pasāriyatīti karuṇā.
rows.english: Or alternatively, it is scattered (kiriyati) upon those who suffer, it is extended to them by pervasion, thus it is compassion (karuṇā).

rows.pali: Modanti tāya taṃsamaṅgino, sayaṃ vā modati, modanamattameva vā tanti muditā.
rows.english: Those endowed with it are glad (modanti), or itself is glad (modati), or it is the mere act of being glad (modana), thus it is gladness (muditā).

rows.pali: "Averā hontū"tiādibyāpārappahānena majjhattabhāvūpagamanena ca upekkhatīti upekkhā.
rows.english: It looks on at (upekkhati), abandoning such interestedness as thinking “May they be free from enmity” and having recourse to neutrality, thus it is equanimity (upekkhā).

rows.pali: 263.Lakkhaṇādito panettha hitākārappavattilakkhaṇā mettā, hitūpasaṃhārarasā, āghātavinayapaccupaṭṭhānā, sattānaṃ manāpabhāvadassanapadaṭṭhānā.
rows.english: [Characteristic, Etc.] 93. As to the characteristic, etc., loving-kindness is characterized here as promoting the aspect of welfare. Its function is to prefer welfare. It is manifested as the removal of annoyance. Its proximate cause is seeing loveableness in beings.

rows.pali: Byāpādūpasamo etissā sampatti, sinehasambhavo vipatti.
rows.english: It succeeds when it makes ill will subside, and it fails when it produces (selfish) affection.

rows.pali: Dukkhāpanayanākārappavattilakkhaṇā karuṇā, paradukkhāsahanarasā, avihiṃsāpaccupaṭṭhānā, dukkhābhibhūtānaṃ anāthabhāvadassanapadaṭṭhānā.
rows.english: 94.Compassion is characterized as promoting the aspect of allaying suffering. Its function resides in not bearing others’ suffering. It is manifested as non- cruelty. Its proximate cause is to see helplessness in those overwhelmed by suffering.

rows.pali: Vihiṃsūpasamo tassā sampatti, sokasambhavo vipatti.
rows.english: It succeeds when it makes cruelty subside and it fails when it produces sorrow.

rows.pali: Pamodanalakkhaṇā muditā, anissāyanarasā, arativighātapaccupaṭṭhānā, sattānaṃ sampattidassanapadaṭṭhānā.
rows.english: 95. Gladness is characterized as gladdening (produced by others’ success).12 Its function resides in being unenvious. It is manifested as the elimination of aversion (boredom). Its proximate cause is seeing beings, success.

rows.pali: Arativūpasamo tassā sampatti, pahāsasambhavo vipatti.
rows.english: It succeeds when it makes aversion (boredom) subside, and it fails when it produces merriment.

rows.pali: Sattesu majjhattākārappavattilakkhaṇā upekkhā, sattesu samabhāvadassanarasā, paṭighānunayavūpasamapaccupaṭṭhānā, "kammassakā sattā, te kassa ruciyā sukhitā vā bhavissanti, dukkhato vā muccissanti, pattasampattito vā na parihāyissantī"ti evaṃ pavattakammassakatādassanapadaṭṭhānā.
rows.english: 96.Equanimity is characterized as promoting the aspect of neutrality towards beings. Its function is to see equality in beings. It is manifested as the quieting of resentment and approval. Its proximate cause is seeing ownership of deeds (kamma) thus: “Beings are owners of their deeds. Whose13 [if not theirs] is the choice by which they will become happy, or will get free from suffering, or will not fall away from the success they have reached?

rows.pali: Paṭighānunayavūpasamo tassā sampatti, gehasitāya aññāṇupekkhāya sambhavo vipatti.
rows.english: ” It succeeds when it makes resentment and approval subside, and it fails when it produces the equanimity of unknowing, which is that [worldly-minded indifference of ignorance] based on the house life.

rows.pali: 264.Catunnampi panetesaṃ brahmavihārānaṃ vipassanāsukhañceva bhavasampatti ca sādhāraṇappayojanaṃ.
rows.english: [Purpose] 97. The general purpose of these four divine abidings is the bliss of insight and an excellent [form of future] existence.

rows.pali: Byāpādādipaṭighāto āveṇikaṃ.
rows.english: That peculiar to each is respectively the warding off of ill will, and so on.

rows.pali: Byāpādapaṭighātappayojanā hettha mettā.
rows.english: For here loving-kindness has the purpose of warding off ill will,

rows.pali: Vihiṃsāaratirāgapaṭighātappayojanā itarā.
rows.english: while the others have the respective purposes of warding off cruelty, aversion (boredom), and greed or resentment.

rows.pali: Vuttampi cetaṃ –
rows.english: And this is said too:

rows.pali: "Nissaraṇañhetaṃ, āvuso, byāpādassa yadidaṃ mettā cetovimutti.
rows.english: “For this is the escape from ill will, friends, that is to say, the mind-deliverance of loving-kindness …

rows.pali: Nissaraṇañhetaṃ, āvuso, vihesāya yadidaṃ karuṇā cetovimutti.
rows.english: For this is the escape from cruelty, friends, that is to say, the mind-deliverance of compassion …

rows.pali: Nissaraṇañhetaṃ, āvuso, aratiyā yadidaṃ muditā cetovimutti.
rows.english: For this is the escape from boredom, friends, that is to say, the mind-deliverance of gladness …

rows.pali: Nissaraṇañhetaṃ, āvuso, rāgassa yadidaṃ upekkhā cetovimuttī"ti (dī. ni. 3.326; a. ni. 6.13).
rows.english: For this is the escape from greed, friends, that is to say, the mind-deliverance of equanimity” (D III 248).

rows.pali: 265.Ekekassa cettha āsannadūravasena dve dve paccatthikā.
rows.english: [The Near and Far Enemies] 98.And here each one has two enemies, one near and one far.

rows.pali: Mettābrahmavihārassa hi samīpacāro viya purisassa sapatto guṇadassanasabhāgatāya rāgo āsannapaccatthiko, so lahuṃ otāraṃ labhati, tasmā tato suṭṭhu mettā rakkhitabbā.
rows.english: The divine abiding of loving-kindness [319] has greed as its near enemy,14 since both share in seeing virtues. Greed behaves like a foe who keeps close by a man, and it easily finds an opportunity. So loving-kindness should be well protected from it.

rows.pali: Pabbatādigahananissito viya purisassa sapatto sabhāgavisabhāgatāya byāpādo dūrapaccatthiko, tasmā tato nibbhayena mettāyitabbaṃ.
rows.english: And ill will, which is dissimilar to the similar greed, is its far enemy like a foe ensconced in a rock wilderness. So loving-kindness must be practiced free from fear of that;

rows.pali: Mettāyissati ca nāma, kopañca karissatīti aṭṭhānametaṃ.
rows.english: for it is not possible to practice loving-kindness and feel anger simultaneously (see D III 247–48).

rows.pali: Karuṇābrahmavihārassa "cakkhuviññeyyānaṃ rūpānaṃ iṭṭhānaṃ kantānaṃ manāpānaṃ manoramānaṃ lokāmisapaṭisaṃyuttānaṃ appaṭilābhaṃ vā appaṭilābhato samanupassato pubbe vā paṭiladdhapubbaṃ atītaṃ niruddhaṃ vipariṇataṃ samanussarato uppajjati domanassaṃ, yaṃ evarūpaṃ domanassaṃ, idaṃ vuccati gehasitaṃ domanassa"ntiādinā (ma. ni. 3.307) nayena āgataṃ gehasitaṃ domanassaṃ vipattidassanasabhāgatāya āsannapaccatthikaṃ.
rows.english: 99.Compassion has grief based on the home life as its near enemy, since both share in seeing failure. Such grief has been described in the way beginning, “When a man either regards as a privation failure to obtain visible objects cognizable by the eye that are sought after, desired, agreeable, gratifying and associated with worldliness, or when he recalls those formerly obtained that are past, ceased and changed, then grief arises in him. Such grief as this is called grief based on the home life” (M III 218).

rows.pali: Sabhāgavisabhāgatāya vihiṃsā dūrapaccatthikā.
rows.english: And cruelty, which is dissimilar to the similar grief, is its far enemy.

rows.pali: Tasmā tato nibbhayena karuṇāyitabbaṃ.
rows.english: So compassion must be practiced free from fear of that;

rows.pali: Karuṇañca nāma karissati, pāṇiādīhi ca viheṭhissatīti aṭṭhānametaṃ.
rows.english: for it is not possible to practice compassion and be cruel to breathing things simultaneously.

rows.pali: Muditābrahmavihārassa "cakkhuviññeyyānaṃ rūpānaṃ iṭṭhānaṃ - pe - lokāmisapaṭisaṃyuttānaṃ paṭilābhaṃ vā paṭilābhato samanupassato pubbe vā paṭiladdhapubbaṃ atītaṃ niruddhaṃ vipariṇataṃ samanussarato uppajjati somanassaṃ, yaṃ evarūpaṃ somanassaṃ, idaṃ vuccati gehasitaṃ somanassa"ntiādinā (ma. ni. 3.306) nayena āgataṃ gehasitaṃ somanassaṃ sampattidassanasabhāgatāya āsannapaccatthikaṃ, sabhāgavisabhāgatāya arati dūrapaccatthikā.
rows.english: 100. Gladness has joy based on the home life as its near enemy, since both share in seeing success. Such joy has been described in the way beginning, “When a man either regards as gain the obtaining of visible objects cognizable by the eye that are sought … and associated with worldliness, or recalls those formerly obtained that are past, ceased, and changed, then joy arises in him. Such joy as this is called joy based on the home life” (M III 217). And aversion (boredom), which is dissimilar to the similar joy, is its far enemy.

rows.pali: Tasmā tato nibbhayena muditā bhāvetabbā.
rows.english: So gladness should be practiced free from fear of that;

rows.pali: Mudito ca nāma bhavissati, pantasenāsanesu ca adhikusalesu dhammesu vā ukkaṇṭhissatīti aṭṭhānametaṃ.
rows.english: for it is not possible to practice gladness and be discontented with remote abodes and things connected with the higher profitableness simultaneously.

rows.pali: Upekkhābrahmavihārassa pana "cakkhunā rūpaṃ disvā uppajjati upekkhā bālassa mūḷhassa puthujjanassa anodhijinassa avipākajinassa anādīnavadassāvino assutavato puthujjanassa yā evarūpā upekkhā, rūpaṃ sā nātivattati.
rows.english: 101. Such unknowing has been described in the way beginning, “On seeing a visible object with the eye equanimity arises in the foolish infatuated ordinary man, in the untaught ordinary man who has not conquered his limitations, who has not conquered future [kamma] result, who is unperceiving of danger. Such equanimity as this does not surmount the visible object.

rows.pali: Tasmā sā upekkhā gehasitāti vuccatī"tiādinā (ma. ni. 3.308) nayena āgatā gehasitā aññāṇupekkhā dosaguṇāvicāraṇavasena sabhāgattā āsannapaccatthikā.
rows.english: Such equanimity as this is called equanimity based on the home life” (M III 219). Equanimity has the equanimity of unknowing (above) based on the home life as its near enemy, since both share in ignoring faults and virtues.

rows.pali: Sabhāgavisabhāgatāya rāgapaṭighā dūrapaccatthikā.
rows.english: And greed and resentment, which are dissimilar to the similar unknowing, are its far enemies.

rows.pali: Tasmā tato nibbhayena upekkhitabbaṃ.
rows.english: Therefore equanimity must be practiced free from fear of that;

rows.pali: Upekkhissati ca nāma, rajjissati ca paṭihaññissati cāti aṭṭhānametaṃ.
rows.english: for it is not possible to look on with equanimity and be inflamed with greed or be resentful15 simultaneously.

rows.pali: 266.Sabbesampi ca etesaṃ kattukāmatā chando ādi, nīvaraṇādivikkhambhanaṃ majjhaṃ, appanā pariyosānaṃ.
rows.english: [The Beginning, Middle and End, Etc.] 102. Now, zeal consisting in desire to act is the beginning of all these things. Suppression of the hindrances, etc., is the middle. Absorption is the end.

rows.pali: Paññattidhammavasena eko vā satto aneke vā sattā ārammaṇaṃ.
rows.english: Their object is a single living being or many living beings, as a mental object consisting in a concept.

rows.pali: Upacāre vā appanāya vā pattāya ārammaṇavaḍḍhanaṃ.
rows.english: [The Order in Extension] 103. The extension of the object takes place either in access or in absorption.

rows.pali: Tatrāyaṃ vaḍḍhanakkamo, yathā hi kusalo kassako kasitabbaṭṭhānaṃ paricchinditvā kasati, evaṃ paṭhamameva ekamāvāsaṃ paricchinditvā tattha sattesu imasmiṃ āvāse sattā averā hontūtiādinā nayena mettā bhāvetabbā.
rows.english: Here is the order of it. Just as a skilled ploughman first delimits an area and then does his ploughing, so first a single dwelling should be delimited and loving- kindness developed towards all beings there in the way beginning, “In this dwelling may all beings be free from enmity.”

rows.pali: Tattha cittaṃ muduṃ kammaniyaṃ katvā dve āvāsā paricchinditabbā.
rows.english: When his mind has become malleable and wieldy with respect to that, he can then delimit two dwellings.

rows.pali: Tato anukkamena tayo, cattāro, pañca, cha, satta, aṭṭha, nava, dasa, ekā racchā, upaḍḍhagāmo, gāmo, janapado, rajjaṃ, ekā disāti evaṃ yāva ekaṃ cakkavāḷaṃ, tato vā pana bhiyyo tattha tattha sattesu mettā bhāvetabbā.
rows.english: Next he can successively delimit three, four, five, six, seven, eight, nine, ten, one street, half the village, the whole village, the district, the kingdom, one direction, and so on up to one world-sphere, or even beyond that, and develop loving- kindness towards the beings in such areas.

rows.pali: Tathā karuṇādayoti ayamettha ārammaṇavaḍḍhanakkamo.
rows.english: Likewise with compassion and so on. This is the order in extending here.

rows.pali: 267.Yathā pana kasiṇānaṃ nissando āruppā, samādhinissando nevasaññānāsaññāyatanaṃ, vipassanānissando phalasamāpatti, samathavipassanānissando nirodhasamāpatti, evaṃ purimabrahmavihārattayanissando ettha upekkhābrahmavihāro.
rows.english: [The Outcome] 104. Just as the immaterial states are the outcome of the kasiṇas, and the base consisting of neither perception nor non-perception is the outcome of concentration, and fruition attainment is the outcome of insight, and the attainment of cessation is the outcome of serenity coupled with insight, so the divine abiding of equanimity is the outcome of the first three divine abidings.

rows.pali: Yathā hi thambhe anussāpetvā tulāsaṅghāṭaṃ anāropetvā na sakkā ākāse kūṭagopānasiyo ṭhapetuṃ, evaṃ purimesu tatiyajjhānaṃ vinā na sakkā catutthaṃ bhāvetunti.
rows.english: For just as the gable rafters cannot be placed in the air without having first set up the scaffolding and built the framework of beams, so it is not possible to develop the fourth (jhāna in the fourth divine abiding) without having already developed the third jhāna in the earlier (three divine abidings).

rows.pali: 268.Ettha siyā, kasmā panetā mettākaruṇāmuditāupekkhā brahmavihārāti vuccanti?
rows.english: [Four Questions] 105. And here it may be asked: But why are loving-kindness, compassion, gladness, and equanimity, called divine abidings?

rows.pali: Kasmā ca catassova?
rows.english: And why are they only four?

rows.pali: Ko ca etāsaṃ kamo, abhidhamme ca kasmā appamaññāti vuttāti?
rows.english: And what is their order? And why are they called measureless states in the Abhidhamma?

rows.pali: Vuccate, seṭṭhaṭṭhena tāva niddosabhāvena cettha brahmavihāratā veditabbā.
rows.english: 106.It may be replied: The divineness of the abiding (brahmavihāratā) should be understood here in the sense of best and in the sense of immaculate.

rows.pali: Sattesu sammāpaṭipattibhāvena hi seṭṭhā ete vihārā.
rows.english: For these abidings are the best in being the right attitude towards beings.

rows.pali: Yathā ca brahmāno niddosacittā viharanti, evaṃ etehi sampayuttā yogino brahmasamā hutvā viharantīti seṭṭhaṭṭhena niddosabhāvena ca brahmavihārāti vuccanti.
rows.english: And just as Brahmā gods abide with immaculate minds, so the meditators who associate themselves with these abidings abide on an equal footing with Brahmā gods. So they are called divine abidings in the sense of best and in the sense of immaculate.

rows.pali: 269.Kasmā ca catassovātiādi pañhassa pana idaṃ vissajjanaṃ.
rows.english: 107. Here are the answers to the questions beginning with “Why are they only four? ”:

rows.pali: Etāsu hi yasmā mettā byāpādabahulassa, karuṇā vihesābahulassa, muditā aratibahulassa, upekkhā rāgabahulassa visuddhimaggo.
rows.english: 108. For among these, loving-kindness is the way to purity for one who has much ill will, compassion is that for one who has much cruelty, gladness is that for one who has much aversion (boredom), and equanimity is that for one who has much greed.

rows.pali: Yasmā ca hitūpasaṃhāraahitāpanayanasampattimodanaanābhogavasena catubbidhoyeva sattesu manasikāro.
rows.english: Also attention given to beings is only fourfold, that is to say, as bringing welfare, as removing suffering, as being glad at their success, and as unconcern, [that is to say, impartial neutrality].

rows.pali: Yasmā ca yathā mātā daharagilānayobbanappattasakiccapasutesu catūsu puttesu daharassa abhivuḍḍhikāmā hoti, gilānassa gelaññāpanayanakāmā, yobbanappattassa yobbanasampattiyā ciraṭṭhitikāmā, sakakiccapasutassa kismiñci pariyāye abyāvaṭā hoti, tathā appamaññāvihārikenāpi sabbasattesu mettādivasena bhavitabbaṃ.
rows.english: And one abiding in the measureless states should practice loving-kindness and the rest like a mother with four sons, namely, a child, an invalid, one in the flush of youth, and one busy with his own affairs; for she wants the child to grow up, wants the invalid to get well, wants the one in the flush of youth to enjoy for long the benefits of youth, and is not at all bothered about the one who is busy with his own affairs.

rows.pali: Tasmā ito visuddhimaggādivasā catassova appamaññā.
rows.english: That is why the measureless states are only four as “due to paths to purity and other sets of four.”

rows.pali: Yasmā catassopetā bhāvetukāmena paṭhamaṃ hitākārappavattivasena sattesu paṭipajjitabbaṃ, hitākārappavattilakkhaṇā ca mettā.
rows.english: 109. One who wants to develop these four should practice them towards beings first as the promotion of the aspect of welfare—and loving-kindness has the promotion of the aspect of welfare as its characteristic;

rows.pali: Tato evaṃ patthitahitānaṃ sattānaṃ dukkhābhibhavaṃ disvā vā sutvā vā sambhāvetvā vā dukkhāpanayanākārappavattivasena, dukkhāpanayanākārappavattilakkhaṇā ca karuṇā.
rows.english: and next, on seeing or hearing or judging16 that beings whose welfare has been thus wished for are at the mercy of suffering, they should be practiced as the promotion of the aspect of the removal of suffering—and compassion has the promotion of the aspect of the removal of suffering as its characteristic;

rows.pali: Athevaṃ patthitahitānaṃ patthitadukkhāpagamānañca nesaṃ sampattiṃ disvā sampattipamodanavasena, pamodanalakkhaṇā ca muditā.
rows.english: and then, on seeing the success of those whose welfare has been wished for and the removal of whose suffering has been wished for, they should be practiced as being glad—and gladness has the act of gladdening as its characteristic;

rows.pali: Tato paraṃ pana kattabbābhāvato ajjhupekkhakattasaṅkhātena majjhattākārena paṭipajjitabbaṃ, majjhattākārappavattilakkhaṇā ca upekkhā.
rows.english: but after that there is nothing to be done and so they should be practiced as the neutral aspect, in other words, the state of an onlooker—and equanimity has the promotion of the aspect of neutrality as its characteristic;

rows.pali: Tasmā ito hitādiākāravasā panāsaṃ paṭhamaṃ mettā vuttā, atha karuṇā muditā upekkhāti ayaṃ kamo veditabbo.
rows.english: therefore, since their respective aims are the aspect of welfare, etc., their order should be understood to correspond, with loving- kindness stated first, then compassion, gladness and equanimity.

rows.pali: Yasmā pana sabbāpetā appamāṇe gocare pavattanti.
rows.english: 110.All of them, however, occur with a measureless scope,

rows.pali: Appamāṇā hi sattā etāsaṃ gocarabhūtā.
rows.english: for their scope is measureless beings;

rows.pali: Ekasattassāpi ca ettake padese mettādayo bhāvetabbāti evaṃ pamāṇaṃ agahetvā sakalapharaṇavaseneva pavattāti.
rows.english: and instead of assuming a measure such as “Loving- kindness, etc., should be developed only towards a single being, or in an area of such an extent,” they occur with universal pervasion.

rows.pali: Tena vuttaṃ –
rows.english: That is why it was said:

rows.pali: 270.Evaṃ appamāṇagocaratāya ekalakkhaṇāsu cāpi etāsu purimā tisso tikacatukkajjhānikāva honti.
rows.english: [As Producing Three Jhānas and Four Jhānas] 111.Though they have a single characteristic in having a measureless scope, yet the first three are only of triple and quadruple jhāna [respectively in the fourfold and fivefold reckonings].

rows.pali: Kasmā?
rows.english: Why?

rows.pali: Somanassāvippayogato.
rows.english: Because they are not dissociated from joy.

rows.pali: Kasmā panāyaṃ somanassena avippayogoti?
rows.english: But why are their aims not dissociated from joy?

rows.pali: Domanassasamuṭṭhitānaṃ byāpādādīnaṃ nissaraṇattā.
rows.english: Because they are the escape from ill will, etc., which are originated by grief.

rows.pali: Pacchimā pana avasesaekajjhānikāva.
rows.english: But the last one belongs only to the remaining single jhāna.

rows.pali: Kasmā?
rows.english: Why?

rows.pali: Upekkhāvedanāsampayogato.
rows.english: Because it is associated with equanimous feeling.

rows.pali: Na hi sattesu majjhattākārappavattā brahmavihārupekkhā upekkhāvedanaṃ vinā vattatīti.
rows.english: For the divine abiding of equanimity that occurs in the aspect of neutrality towards beings does not exist apart from equanimous [that is to say, neither- painful-nor-pleasant] feeling.

rows.pali: 271.Yo panevaṃ vadeyya "yasmā bhagavatā aṭṭhakanipāte catūsupi appamaññāsu avisesena vuttaṃ 'tato tvaṃ bhikkhu imaṃ samādhiṃ savitakkampi savicāraṃ bhāveyyāsi, avitakkampi vicāramattaṃ bhāveyyāsi, avitakkampi avicāraṃ bhāveyyāsi, sappītikampi bhāveyyāsi, nippītikampi bhāveyyāsi, sātasahagatampi bhāveyyāsi, upekkhāsahagatampi bhāveyyāsī'ti (a. ni. 8.63), tasmā catasso appamaññā catukkapañcakajjhānikā"ti.
rows.english: 112. However, someone might say this: “It has been said by the Blessed One in the Book of Eights, speaking of the measureless states in general: ‘Next, bhikkhu, you should develop the concentration with applied thought and sustained thought, and you should develop it without applied thought and with sustained thought only, and you should develop it without applied thought and without sustained thought, and you should develop it with happiness, and you should develop it without happiness, and you should develop it accompanied by gratification, and you should develop it accompanied by equanimity’ (A IV 300). Consequently all four measureless states have quadruple and quintuple jhāna.”

rows.pali: So māhevantissa vacanīyo.
rows.english: 113. He should be told: “Do not put it like that.

rows.pali: Evañhi sati kāyānupassanādayopi catukkapañcakajjhānikā siyuṃ, vedanādīsu ca paṭhamajjhānampi natthi, pageva dutiyādīni.
rows.english: For if that were so, then contemplation of the body, etc., would also have quadruple and quintuple jhāna. But there is not even the first jhāna in the contemplation of feeling or in the other two.17

rows.pali: Tasmā byañjanacchāyāmattaṃ gahetvā mā bhagavantaṃ abbhācikkhi, gambhīraṃ hi buddhavacanaṃ, taṃ ācariye payirupāsitvā adhippāyato gahetabbaṃ.
rows.english: So do not misrepresent the Blessed One by adherence to the letter. The Enlightened One’s word is profound and should be taken as it is intended, giving due weight to the teachers.”

rows.pali: 272.Ayañhi tatrādhippāyo – "sādhu me, bhante, bhagavā saṃkhittena dhammaṃ desetu, yamahaṃ bhagavato dhammaṃ sutvā eko vūpakaṭṭho appamatto ātāpī pahitatto vihareyya"nti evaṃ āyācitadhammadesanaṃ kira taṃ bhikkhuṃ yasmā so pubbepi dhammaṃ sutvā tattheva vasati, na samaṇadhammaṃ kātuṃ gacchati, tasmā naṃ bhagavā "evameva panidhekacce moghapurisā mamaññeva ajjhesanti, dhamme ca bhāsite mamaññeva anubandhitabbaṃ maññantī"ti apasādetvā puna yasmā so arahattassa upanissayasampanno, tasmā naṃ ovadanto āha – "tasmātiha te bhikkhu evaṃ sikkhitabbaṃ, ajjhattaṃ me cittaṃ ṭhitaṃ bhavissati susaṇṭhitaṃ, na cuppannā pāpakā akusalā dhammā cittaṃ pariyādāya ṭhassantīti.
rows.english: 114. And the intention here is this: The Blessed One, it seems, was asked to teach the Dhamma thus: “Venerable sir, it would be good if the Blessed One would teach me the Dhamma in brief, so that, having heard the Blessed One’s Dhamma, I may dwell alone, withdrawn, diligent, ardent and self-exerted” (A IV 299). But the Blessed One had no confidence yet in that bhikkhu, since although he had already heard the Dhamma he had nevertheless gone on living there instead of going to do the ascetic’s duties, [and the Blessed One expressed his lack of confidence] thus: “So too, some misguided men merely question me, and when the Dhamma is expounded [to them], they still fancy that they need not follow me” (A IV 299). However, the bhikkhu had the potentiality for the attainment of Arahantship, and so he advised him again, [323] saying: “Therefore, bhikkhu, you should train thus: ‘My mind shall be steadied, quite steadied internally, and arisen evil unprofitable things shall not obsess my mind and remain.’

rows.pali: Evañhi te bhikkhu sikkhitabba"nti.
rows.english: You should train thus” (A IV 299).

rows.pali: Iminā panassa ovādena niyakajjhattavasena cittekaggatāmatto mūlasamādhi vutto.
rows.english: But what is stated in that advice is basic concentration consisting in mere unification of mind18 internally in the sense of in oneself (see Ch. XIV, n. 75).

rows.pali: Tato "ettakeneva santuṭṭhiṃ anāpajjitvā evaṃ so eva samādhi vaḍḍhetabbo"ti dassetuṃ "yato kho te bhikkhu ajjhattaṃ cittaṃ ṭhitaṃ hoti susaṇṭhitaṃ, na cuppannā pāpakā akusalā dhammā cittaṃ pariyādāya tiṭṭhanti.
rows.english: 115. After that he told him about its development by means of loving-kindness in order to show that he should not rest content with just that much but should intensify his basic concentration in this way: “As soon as your mind has become steadied, quite steadied internally, bhikkhu, and arisen evil unprofitable things do not obsess your mind and remain,

rows.pali: Tato te bhikkhu evaṃ sikkhitabbaṃ mettā me cetovimutti bhāvitā bhavissati bahulīkatā yānikatā vatthukatā anuṭṭhitā paricitā susamāraddhāti.
rows.english: then you should train thus: ‘The mind- deliverance of loving-kindness will be developed by me, frequently practiced, made the vehicle, made the foundation, established, consolidated, and properly undertaken.’

rows.pali: Evañhi te bhikkhu sikkhitabba"nti evamassa mettāvasena bhāvanaṃ vatvā puna "yato kho te bhikkhu ayaṃ samādhi evaṃ bhāvito hoti bahulīkato, tato tvaṃ bhikkhu imaṃ mūlasamādhiṃ savitakkampi savicāraṃ bhāveyyāsi - pe - upekkhāsahagatampi bhāveyyāsī"ti vuttaṃ.
rows.english: You should train thus, bhikkhu” (A IV 299–300), after which he said further: “As soon as this concentration has been thus developed by you, bhikkhu,19 and frequently practiced, then you should develop this concentration with applied thought and sustained thought … and you should develop it accompanied by equanimity” (A IV 300).

rows.pali: Tassattho – yadā te bhikkhu ayaṃ mūlasamādhi evaṃ mettāvasena bhāvito hoti, tadā tvaṃ tāvatakenāpi tuṭṭhiṃ anāpajjitvāva imaṃ mūlasamādhiṃ aññesupi ārammaṇesu catukkapañcakajjhānāni pāpayamāno savitakkampi savicārantiādinā nayena bhāveyyāsīti.
rows.english: 116. The meaning is this: “Bhikkhu, when this basic concentration has been developed by you by means of loving-kindness, then, instead of resting content with just that much, you should make this basic concentration reach quadruple and quintuple jhāna in other objects by [further] developing it in the way beginning ‘With applied thought.’ ”

rows.pali: Evaṃ vatvā ca puna karuṇādiavasesabrahmavihārapubbaṅgamampissa aññesu ārammaṇesu catukkapañcakajjhānavasena bhāvanaṃ kareyyāsīti dassento "yato kho te bhikkhu ayaṃ samādhi evaṃ bhāvito hoti bahulīkato.
rows.english: 117. And having spoken thus, pointing out that “you should effect its [further] development by means of quadruple and quintuple jhāna in other objects, this [further] development being preceded by the remaining divine abidings of compassion and the rest.” he further said: “As soon as this concentration has been thus developed by you, bhikkhu, and frequently practiced,

rows.pali: Tato te bhikkhu evaṃ sikkhitabbaṃ karuṇā me cetovimuttī"tiādimāha.
rows.english: then you should train thus: ‘The mind-deliverance of compassion will be developed by me …’ (A IV 300), etc.

rows.pali: Evaṃ mettādipubbaṅgamaṃ catukkapañcakajjhānavasena bhāvanaṃ dassetvā puna kāyānupassanādipubbaṅgamaṃ dassetuṃ "yato kho te bhikkhu ayaṃ samādhi evaṃ bhāvito hoti bahulīkato, tato te bhikkhu evaṃ sikkhitabbaṃ kāye kāyānupassī viharissāmī"ti ādiṃ vatvā "yato kho te bhikkhu ayaṃ samādhi evaṃ bhāvito bhavissati subhāvito, tato tvaṃ bhikkhu yena yeneva gagghasi, phāsuññeva gagghasi, yattha yattheva ṭhassasi, phāsuññeva ṭhassasi, yattha yattheva nisīdissasi, phāsuññeva nisīdissasi, yattha yattheva seyyaṃ kappessasi, phāsuññeva seyyaṃ kappessasī"ti arahattanikūṭena desanaṃ samāpesi.
rows.english: 118. Having thus shown how its [further] development by means of quadruple and quintuple jhāna is preceded by loving-kindness, etc., and having told him, “As soon as this concentration has been developed by you, bhikkhu, and frequently practiced, then you should train thus: ‘I shall dwell contemplating the body as a body,’” etc., he concluded the discourse with Arahantship as its culmination thus: “As soon as this concentration has been developed by you, bhikkhu, completely developed, then wherever you go you will go in comfort, wherever you stand you will stand in comfort, wherever [324] you sit you will sit in comfort, wherever you make your couch you will do so in comfort” (A IV 301).

rows.pali: Tasmā tikacatukkajjhānikāva mettādayo, upekkhā pana avasesaekajjhānikāvāti veditabbā.
rows.english: From that it must be understood that the [three] beginning with loving-kindness have only triple-quadruple jhāna, and that equanimity has only the single remaining jhāna.

rows.pali: Tatheva ca abhidhamme (dha. sa. 251 ādayo; vibha. 673 ādayo) vibhattāti.
rows.english: And they are expounded in the same way in the Abhidhamma as well.

rows.pali: 273.Evaṃ tikacatukkajjhānavasena ceva avasesaekajjhānavasena ca dvidhā ṭhitānampi etāsaṃ subhaparamādivasena aññamaññaṃ asadiso ānubhāvaviseso veditabbo.
rows.english: [The Highest Limit of Each] 119. And while they are twofold by way of the triple-quadruple jhāna and the single remaining jhāna, still they should be understood to be distinguishable in each case

rows.pali: Haliddavasanasuttasmiṃ hi etā subhaparamādibhāvena visesetvā vuttā.
rows.english: by a different efficacy consisting in having “beauty as the highest,” etc. For they are so described in the Haliddavasana Sutta,

rows.pali: Yathāha – "subhaparamāhaṃ, bhikkhave, mettaṃ cetovimuttiṃ vadāmi.
rows.english: according as it is said: “Bhikkhus, the mind-deliverance of loving-kindness has beauty as the highest, I say …
